source.title: Introduce GPU ephemeral backup tfmodule and tfstate anchoring helper
source.author: Priya Nair
source.release_notes: Add new terraform module 'gpu_ephemeral_backup' for short-lived snapshot/restore hooks during nodepool upgrades; add 'tfstate_anchor' helper to create cryptographically-signed state checkpoints for safer rollbacks. No user-facing API changes; opt-in via module inclusion.
source.description:
Background and motivation:
This PR introduces two small, composable terraform modules to reduce operational risk during Private installations that use GPU node pools and sharded tfstate layouts. We've seen recurring incidents where control-plane drift or partial apply failures during nodepool upgrades required manual state surgeries (ENG-4821). The goal is to provide a repeatable, automated safety net: ephemeral backups of nodepool metadata and a lightweight tfstate anchoring helper that produces a signed checkpoint file distributed to the ops bucket.

What this PR does (summary):
- Adds modules/gpu_ephemeral_backup: an opt-in module that creates a snapshot of the nodepool control metadata, saves kubelet/CRI config snippets, and records a lightweight manifest (node labels, taints, instance-ids) to the configured artifacts bucket. Designed for very short retention (24–72h) to minimize storage and compliance exposure.
- Adds modules/tfstate_anchor: helper that, when invoked, creates a signed checkpoint object (HMAC using a KMS key) that includes the backend state version and a checksum of critical tf resources; intended as a checkpoint for emergency rollbacks and as a hook for the tfstate-migrator workflow.
- Wire-up examples in examples/private/gpu-upgrade-safety which demonstrate using both modules around a nodepool upgrade plan (pre-apply snapshot, post-apply anchor).
- Tests and CI: adds terratest-style integration snippets and unit validation via tflint/tfsec CI gates.

Why this is different from existing state helpers: 
- Lightweight: we do not duplicate full tfstate snapshots (those are expensive/slow). Instead we capture a minimal, verifiable checkpoint that is sufficient to detect drift and reconstruct resource pointers for the tfstate reconciler.
- Kubernetes-aware: the backup manifest captures node-level Kubernetes metadata necessary to reattach pods/stateful-resources to replacement nodes.
- Designed for Private: integrates with our private artifacts bucket patterns, KMS key usage, and tenancy-scoped IAM roles.

Files changed (high-level):
- modules/gpu_ephemeral_backup/main.tf (new)
- modules/gpu_ephemeral_backup/outputs.tf (new)
- modules/gpu_ephemeral_backup/variables.tf (new)
- modules/tfstate_anchor/main.tf (new)
- modules/tfstate_anchor/README.md (new)
- examples/private/gpu-upgrade-safety/README.md (new)
- examples/private/gpu-upgrade-safety/main.tf (new)
- ci/terraform/lint.yml (updated to run tflint on new modules)
- tests/integration/gpu_ephemeral_backup_test.go (new terratest stub)

Commits (high-level):
1) init: add gpu_ephemeral_backup module skeleton and basic manifest writer
2) anchor: implement tfstate_anchor with KMS-signed checkpoint generation and helper outputs
3) examples & docs: add example deployment and README guidance for upgrade hooks
4) ci & tests: add tflint/tfsec CI changes and a terratest stub; fix lint issues raised during CI

Design notes and implementation details:
- The ephemeral backup module is intentionally small: it runs a single provider-backed data collection step that queries cloud provider instance metadata and the kube API (via optional provider credentials) and marshals a manifest object to an artifacts bucket. It exposes a short-lived pre-signed URL and a retention TTL.
- The tfstate_anchor module computes a canonical digest (sorted keys) of the supplied critical resources (map input) and writes a JSON checkpoint to the artifacts bucket. A KMS HMAC key (or HSM-backed symmetric key) is used to sign the payload; signature and signer metadata are included in outputs. Consumers (tfstate-migrator, upgrade-runner) can validate anchors before performing destructive operations.
- Both modules are usage-optional and can be wired into existing plan/apply pipelines in private deployments. We do not alter any existing core tfstate backends by default.

Security and compliance considerations:
- Checkpoints contain metadata but do NOT include full credentials or secrets. Any optional kube API access is performed via the provided role/credentials; default behavior is metadata-only.
- Artifacts bucket ACLs follow existing private deploy patterns; the module requires a kms_key resource ARN and a role that can sign/verify.
- Retention defaults to 24 hours but is configurable to meet customer compliance needs.

Testing and validation performed:
- Local terratest run against a small dev project (mocked provider) validated manifest shape and signature validation flow.
- CI added tflint rules and tfsec suppressions with justifications in README.
- Manual validation with the tfstate-migrator dev harness: created anchor -> deliberately induced partial apply -> run migrator validation -> verified anchor prevented unsafe rollback without explicit acknowledge.

Migration/upgrade path and rollback plan:
- This is opt-in. Existing Private installs will not be affected unless the module is included in their private deployment stacks.
- Recommended rollout: include gpu_ephemeral_backup and tfstate_anchor in canary tenant stacks for one week; validate creation of artifacts and signature verification; then expand to remaining tenants.
- In case of problems the modules can be removed without impacting core state backends; artifacts are purely supplemental.

Review thread (summary):
- Ava Chen: asked for stronger validation on the manifest schema and a unit test for missing fields; requested the retention default be 24h instead of 7d for security.
- Author reply: added a JSON schema validation, added test cases, and updated default retention to 24h (see commit 3).
- Diego Morales: asked for explicit IAM permission docs and a suggestion to avoid broad storage ACLs.
- Author reply: added IAM role policy snippet in README and switched to bucket-level objects with signer-only put permissions.
- Liam O'Connor: ran a small integration using the private installer dev cluster and requested a minor rename of output keys for consistency; author updated outputs in commit 4.
- Final: reviewers approved and requested small followup to add production terratest scenarios later.

CI history and resolution:
- Initial pipeline: tflint reported two stylistic issues and tfsec flagged an allowed IAM pattern; CI failed on first run.
- Fixes: corrected tflint warnings, added tfsec suppressions with rationale, updated lint workflow. Final CI green on run #3.

Operational notes for SREs/Field engineers:
- When enabling the modules in a tenant, ensure the artifacts bucket uses lifecycle rules and that KMS keys are rotated per org policy.
- The anchor verification command (scripts/verify_anchor.sh) is included in the examples directory; use it to validate anchors before applying rollbacks.

Follow-ups (future work):
- Add a small operator runbook to automatically reconcile artifacts cleanup post-upgrade.
- Integrate anchor validation as a pre-check in the private-installer upgrade controller.
- Expand terratest coverage to a production-like environment and add a scheduled job that validates anchor creation.

TL;DR: Adds two opt-in terraform helpers to make GPU nodepool upgrades and tfstate-boundary migrations safer by providing short-lived backups and cryptographically-signed checkpoint anchors. Opt-in, backwards-compatible, and CI-tested.
source.notes: Author: Priya Nair. This PR was merged after CI fixes and two approvals. Marked non-breaking and opt-in for customers.

source.title: Marketplace provisioning: IAM + webhook config for entitlement sync (least privilege)
source.author: Ethan Park
source.description: Context: Marketplace-driven customers need a reliable subscription → entitlement → org provisioning flow. The control-plane entitlement sync service now consumes entitlement signals from both AWS Marketplace (API polling + optional notifications) and GCP Marketplace (vendor webhook). This PR adds Terraform-managed IAM, network, and webhook plumbing needed by the entitlement sync services while keeping permissions least-privilege and auditable. Changes: (1) AWS IAM: new dedicated IAM role for entitlement-sync with scoped permissions to aws-marketplace entitlement + metering APIs; separate role for webhook verifier to read secrets and write limited logs/metrics; explicit deny for broad S3 and KMS actions; (2) Webhook ingress: add an internal ALB listener rule + path-based routing for /marketplace/gcp/webhook to the entitlement-sync service target group; add WAF rule set and request size limits; (3) Secrets and key usage: provision an SSM parameter + Secrets Manager secret for GCP webhook signing key rotation, and KMS grants scoped to decrypt only those secrets; (4) Observability + safety: cloudwatch alarms for 5xx spikes on the webhook path, and metric filters for signature verification failures; (5) Documentation: inline least-privilege notes and a short README section on how to validate in staging. Notes: This does not change application logic—only infra wiring and policy boundaries. Follow-up: once we confirm partner testing, we can enable optional SNS-based AWS notifications in prod (module is included but off by default).
source.commits_summary: 1) terraform: add entitlement-sync IAM role + scoped marketplace permissions 2) terraform: add secrets manager + kms grants for webhook signing key 3) terraform: add ALB routing and WAF rules for /marketplace/gcp/webhook 4) terraform: add cloudwatch alarms/metric filters for webhook failures 5) docs: update ops README for staging validation steps
source.changed_areas: Infrastructure modules updated: marketplace-entitlement-sync, iam-roles, alb-routing, waf, secrets, monitoring. Notable files: modules/marketplace-entitlement-sync/iam.tf; modules/marketplace-entitlement-sync/alb.tf; modules/security/waf_marketplace.tf; modules/observability/alarms_marketplace.tf; environments/staging/marketplace.tf; environments/prod/marketplace.tf; docs/marketplace-entitlement-sync.md.
source.notable_snippets: IAM policy excerpt (entitlement-sync role): Allow marketplace entitlement reads: "action": ["aws-marketplace:ResolveCustomer", "aws-marketplace:GetEntitlements"], "resource": "*" (AWS Marketplace APIs do not support resource-level scoping). Allow metering only for our product codes via condition: "aws-marketplace:BatchMeterUsage" with Condition StringEquals "aws:PrincipalTag/Product": "marketplace". Secrets decrypt constrained: kms:Decrypt only when encryption context matches SecretsManager ARNs used by the webhook key. ALB route: path-pattern "/marketplace/gcp/webhook" → target group entitlement-sync; WAF: allowlist Redwood partner IP ranges in staging, signature verification failures rate-limited, body size capped at 64KB.
source.test_plan: Terraform plan/apply in staging workspace; verify: (1) entitlement-sync task role assumes correctly and can call AWS Marketplace GetEntitlements in staging; (2) GCP webhook hits /marketplace/gcp/webhook returns 200 on valid signature, 401 on invalid signature; (3) alarms created and in OK state; (4) WAF blocks oversized payloads; (5) secrets rotation does not require a redeploy (read on request).
source.rollout_plan: Staging apply immediately after merge; run partner webhook replay tests; enable prod ALB listener rule and WAF in guarded mode (count) for 24h; then switch WAF rules to block; keep optional AWS SNS notifications disabled in prod until partner validation completes.
source.security_considerations: Least-privilege: separate roles for sync vs webhook verification; no wildcard admin permissions; KMS decrypt limited to specific secrets; no write access to customer data stores granted here. Webhook hardening: WAF + rate limiting + request size cap; signature key stored in Secrets Manager; logs include request IDs only (no payload logging).
review_conversation: Marcus Lin (Security): AWS Marketplace permissions are necessarily resource="*"; can we add a guardrail to prevent accidental broadening to unrelated AWS APIs? Author: added explicit deny for s3:* and kms:* outside the specific secrets and added a policy lint note in module README.

Cole Summers (Billing/Metering): metering calls should be scoped to product code conditions—do we tag the role or use condition keys? Author: added PrincipalTag Product=marketplace and updated task role tags in ECS module; added note that metering is condition-scoped and validated via IAM policy simulator.

Naomi Feldman (Compliance): please ensure we don’t log webhook payloads in ALB access logs / app logs by default. Author: confirmed ALB access logs only include request metadata; no app payload logging configured in infra; added log redaction note to docs.

Priya Natarajan (Platform): staging-only partner IP allowlist is good; ensure prod doesn’t accidentally inherit. Author: moved allowlist to env var toggle; prod defaults to off; terraform validation added to prevent enabling in prod without explicit override.
source.merge_outcome: Merged via squash; applied to staging within 2 hours post-merge (runbook: ops/marketplace-entitlement-sync.md).
source.release_notes: Marketplace entitlement sync infra now has dedicated least-privilege IAM roles and hardened webhook ingress for GCP entitlement events; adds alarms for webhook error rates and signature failures.

source.title: perf-canary: wire service account, secret storage, and least-privilege IAM
source.author: Nadia Rahman
source.body:
Summary: This PR adds the Terraform wiring needed to deploy perf-canary safely: (1) a dedicated service account with least-privilege IAM, (2) secret storage + access policies for canary auth, and (3) environment/region guardrails to prevent accidental fanout. This unblocks the perf-canary Helm chart rollout by providing a constrained identity that can call internal inference endpoints and emit metrics without broad access.

Context: perf-canary runs synthetic workloads against internal inference endpoints and publishes performance metrics/alerts. We need a dedicated identity for the canary deployment (no shared runtime/telemetry SA), scoped to: read-only inference invoke, write-only metrics, and read-only baseline artifact access. We also need to store the canary auth token / client secret in managed secret storage and allow only the canary workload identity to decrypt/access it.

What changed (high level): - Introduced perf_canary_service_account module (GCP) and associated IAM bindings. - Added Secret Manager secrets for perf-canary auth material and a KMS policy to allow decrypt only by the canary SA. - Added explicit IAM conditions and narrowed resource scopes (project/namespace/cluster) to avoid granting broad viewer/editor roles. - Added a small set of env/region toggles (enable_perf_canary, perf_canary_regions allowlist) and validation to avoid enabling canary in all regions by mistake.

IAM scope (intended): - Inference invoke: allow calling internal inference gateway (limited audience/scope; no general service-to-service). - Metrics: allow write to telemetry ingest (no read, no admin). - Baselines: allow read from baseline bucket/prefix only (no list on entire bucket, no write). - Secrets: allow access to perf-canary secret versions only (no create/delete; no wildcard).

Operational notes: - Secret rotation: this PR creates the secret resources and IAM; rotation procedures remain in the infra runbook. - Rollback: disabling perf-canary is done by setting enable_perf_canary=false in the environment tfvars; IAM resources remain but are inert.

Testing/verification: - terraform validate + fmt - terraform plan for staging-us-east and prod-us-east: no unexpected diffs outside perf-canary resources - Manual check: SA cannot list other secrets or buckets; can read only the one secret and baseline prefix.

Risk: Medium. IAM changes are production-impacting; however this PR only adds new bindings/resources (no removal of existing roles). Reviewers: please sanity-check IAM conditions and secret access boundaries.

title: perf-canary: wire service account, secret storage, and least-privilege IAM
preview:
Summary: This PR adds the Terraform wiring needed to deploy perf-canary safely: (1) a dedicated service account with least-privilege IAM, (2) secret storage + access policies for canary auth, and (3) environment/region guardrails to prevent accidental fanout. This unblocks the perf-canary Helm chart rollout by providing a constrained identity that can call internal inference endpoints and emit metrics without broad access.

Context: perf-canary runs synthetic workloads against internal inference endpoints and publishes performance metrics/alerts. We need a dedicated identity for the canary deployment (no shared runtime/telemetry SA), scoped to: read-only inference invoke, write-only metrics, and read-only baseline artifact access. We also need to store the canary auth token / client secret in managed secret storage and allow only the canary workload identity to decrypt/access it.

What changed (high level): - Introduced perf_canary_service_account module (GCP) and associated IAM bindings. - Added Secret Manager secrets for perf-canary auth material and a KMS policy to allow decrypt only by the canary SA. - Added explicit IAM conditions and narrowed resource scopes (project/namespace/cluster) to avoid granting broad viewer/editor roles. - Added a small set of env/region toggles (enable_perf_canary, perf_canary_regions allowlist) and validation to avoid enabling canary in all regions by mistake.

IAM scope (intended): - Inference invoke: allow calling internal inference gateway (limited audience/scope; no general service-to-service). - Metrics: allow write to telemetry ingest (no read, no admin). - Baselines: allow read from baseline bucket/prefix only (no list on entire bucket, no write). - Secrets: allow access to perf-canary secret versions only (no create/delete; no wildcard).

Operational notes: - Secret rotation: this PR creates the secret resources and IAM; rotation procedures remain in the infra runbook. - Rollback: disabling perf-canary is done by setting enable_perf_canary=false in the environment tfvars; IAM resources remain but are inert.

Testing/verification: - terraform validate + fmt - terraform plan for staging-us-east and prod-us-east: no unexpected diffs outside perf-canary resources - Manual check: SA cannot list other secrets or buckets; can read only the one secret and baseline prefix.

Risk: Medium. IAM changes are production-impacting; however this PR only adds new bindings/resources (no removal of existing roles). Reviewers: please sanity-check IAM conditions and secret access boundaries.

Follow-ups: - Consider adding org-policy constraint to block secret access from non-approved service accounts (separate PR) - If we move baselines from GCS to config service, we should replace the bucket read binding accordingly.

Refs: Linear ENG-4879 (Deployment helm + terraform), ENG-4872 (MVP), ENG-4878 (alerting routing). Jira INT-2213 (service account permissions request).

source.title: DNS TTL tuning + routing primitives for faster regional failover
source.author: Victor Alvarez
source.body:
Summary: This PR updates Terraform for Redwood’s cross-region traffic steering so regional failover can converge faster and more predictably during impairment. The main change is lowering DNS TTLs for the API entrypoints used by Hosted API and Dedicated (where applicable), while adding guardrails to avoid unintended traffic shifts (provider caching variance, resolver behavior, and over-eager health checks). It also adds explicit routing primitives (health checks + failover policy wiring + outputs for the failover controller) so the automation controller can rely on consistent infra definitions across environments.

Motivation / context: Regional failover automation upgrades (Reliability Program) require that DNS-based steering not be the long pole. Today, several records use TTLs that are appropriate for steady-state but extend failover time, and some resources are defined inconsistently across regions/environments. We want a standard, reviewable baseline that aligns with the failover signal hierarchy and avoids oscillation during partial outages.

What changed: 1) TTL tuning: Reduce TTLs for public API endpoints (api.redwoodinference.com + region aliases) from 60s -> 20s in prod (staging remains 10s for drills), and for dedicated customer vanity domains that opt into Redwood-managed DNS from 300s -> 60s (no change for customer-managed DNS). TTL changes are feature-flagged by Terraform variables so we can stage rollout per region.

2) Health check plumbing: Add/standardize health checks that match the failover controller’s reachability signal (HTTP /healthz/edge and TCP fallback where HTTP is blocked). Health checks include conservative request interval/thresholds to reduce false positives, and are intentionally decoupled from application-level p95 latency signals (those come from metrics; DNS health should only answer “is the edge reachable”).

3) Failover routing policy wiring: Introduce a module that creates/updates the primary/secondary routing policy for regional records (us-east-1 primary, eu-west-1 secondary for the first phase), with explicit “hold-down” notes in tags/annotations for operators. This does not implement automated flips by itself; it ensures the underlying DNS objects exist and are consistent.

4) Outputs for automation + observability: Export record IDs, health check IDs, and key tags as Terraform outputs so the failover controller / incident-bot can link infra objects to audits and dashboards. Added standard tags: reliability_program=regional_failover_automation, service=api-edge, env, region, and owner.

Non-goals: - No provider migration or multi-cloud work.
- No change to customer contract language or SLA.
- No change to app-side routing logic; this is infra-only.

Risk assessment: Lower TTLs can increase query volume and expose resolver caching variance; health checks can cause unintended failover if they’re too sensitive. To mitigate, TTL reductions are staged per region with explicit rollback vars, health checks are conservative, and we keep a minimum TTL floor (20s prod, 10s staging). This PR also avoids introducing any “latency-based” DNS routing to prevent oscillation; failover remains an explicit policy decision via controller logic.

Rollout plan: - Stage 1 (staging): Apply changes in staging and run a rehearsal with the game day checklist. Validate that record propagation and resolver behavior match expectations.
- Stage 2 (prod, limited blast radius): Enable TTL 20s for api.redwoodinference.com in us-east-1/eu-west-1 only. Monitor DNS query rate, NXDOMAIN/servfail rates, edge reachability probes, and error budgets.
- Stage 3 (expand): Roll out to additional regions after the first controlled failover game day confirms success criteria.

Validation: - terraform plan reviewed for prod/us-east-1 and prod/eu-west-1 (no unintended resource recreation).
- Confirmed Route53 health checks are stable in staging under induced partial impairment.
- Confirmed record set changes are in-place updates (no delete/recreate for main A/AAAA alias records).

References: - Linear: ENG-2411 (program), ENG-2418 (signals/alerts), ENG-2421 (game day #1)
- Slack: DNS TTL change risk assessment thread (eng-infra)
- Game day objective: shrink DNS convergence time so controller-driven failover isn’t gated by 60s+ TTLs.

Notes for on-call: If we observe unexpected traffic shifts, rollback is a Terraform var flip restoring TTLs to previous values and disabling the new failover module for affected records. See runbook references in ENG-2421 checklist for monitoring queries and edge reachability during change windows.
commits: terraform(route53): standardize api edge record sets and tags
terraform: add module dns_failover_routing (primary/secondary + health checks)
terraform: tune TTLs for hosted api and opt-in dedicated zones (staged vars)
docs: add operator notes + rollback variables to README for dns module
chore: export record/healthcheck IDs for controller/audit linkage
checks: checkov: pass (2 skipped with justification: CKV_AWS_51 on health check request interval; documented)
review_conversation: Miles Thornton (review): Can we ensure health checks don’t flip regions based on transient 5xx from app deploys? Prefer edge reachability only.

Victor Alvarez (author): Yep — health checks hit /healthz/edge on the gateway and we’re not using latency-based routing. Interval 30s, failure_threshold 3. Added comments + tags clarifying this is reachability-only.
Irene Choi (review): TTL 20s in prod seems aggressive; any floor and rollback path? Also confirm no alias record recreation.

Victor Alvarez (author): Added ttl_floor_prod=20 and a per-env var to revert to previous TTLs. Plan shows in-place updates for record sets (no recreation). Also added a staged rollout note in module README.
Emily Zhao (review): Health check protocol: add TCP fallback for customers that block HTTP at edge? Some networks might drop HTTP checks.
Victor Alvarez (author): Added optional tcp_fallback_enabled; default off in prod but on in staging drills. We can enable per zone if needed. Included in outputs for the controller.
Rafael Mendes (review): Please add outputs that incident-bot can link to for audits (record IDs/healthcheck IDs).
Victor Alvarez (author): Added outputs + standard tags. Devon’s incident-bot can consume these via the controller integration.
Irene Choi (approval): Approved with staged rollout + explicit rollback variables in docs.

source.title: Refresh Terraform modules CI/CD pipeline and integration tests
source.author: Sahana Iyer
source.description: Context: Our Confluence CI/CD page for redwood-terraform describes the original alpha pipeline. Since then we’ve added more modules/examples and the integration suite has been flakier and harder to reason about (provider init duplication, inconsistent path filtering, unclear when to run AWS ephemeral tests). This PR refreshes the GitHub Actions workflows and updates the repo’s CI guidance to reflect the current reality. Summary of changes: (1) PR validation now uses a single module/example discovery step that drives a matrix for fmt/validate/tflint and avoids re-running init unnecessarily. (2) Security scanning: keep Checkov as the baseline gate but also upload Trivy IaC (config) results to SARIF for additional signal; Checkov remains the merge-blocking policy for now. (3) Integration tests: previously nightly + manual; now we also auto-run integration tests on PRs when risk classifier flags high-risk changes (NAT/routing, endpoints/DNS, IAM boundaries, SG egress posture). Low/medium risk PRs remain PR-fast by default. (4) AWS auth for integration tests migrated to aws-actions/configure-aws-credentials@v4 with tighter session policies and explicit region allowlist; still OIDC, no long-lived keys. (5) Release workflow tightened: requires the latest integration run on main within the last 24h OR a release-triggered integration run attached to the release commit; adds a job summary section that captures Terraform + provider versions used for the release. Details (aligns with existing doc): Purpose: CI/CD pipeline for redwood-terraform modules. Goals: consistent fmt/validate/lint/security scanning; catch breaking networking/IAM changes early; predictable SemVer releases for pilot pinning. Non-goals unchanged: not deploying Redwood Private, AWS only, no long-lived envs. Required checks (PR): terraform fmt -check -recursive; terraform validate per changed module/example with plugin cache; tflint with AWS ruleset; checkov against modules/ + examples/ (SARIF upload); docs/examples sanity (validate canonical examples + README drift check); provider/version pin enforcement (Terraform matches .terraform-version; providers pinned; fail on new unpinned providers). Approval policy unchanged in spirit: min 2 approvals; Security Eng required for IAM/logging/egress/endpoint-policy changes; CODEOWNERS paths updated to include new modules/iam-boundaries and modules/vpc-endpoints. Integration tests (ephemeral AWS): still provision/assert/destroy in CI sandbox account, but now triggered by risk classifier for high-risk PRs + nightly + manual + release. Guardrails: SCP region allowlist; deny public S3; budget alarms. Cost controls: workflow timeout; smaller footprints; prefer endpoints over NAT where possible. Troubleshooting notes updated: most common failures now are endpoint eventual consistency and IAM propagation; we added retries to specific asserts and ensured destroy runs even after assert failures. This PR intentionally does not remove Checkov or change the exception policy semantics; it adds Trivy as a non-blocking signal and consolidates workflow structure for speed and clarity.
commits: ci: consolidate terraform init/validate into discovery-driven matrix
ci: add risk classifier for integration test auto-trigger on PRs
ci: add Trivy IaC SARIF upload (non-blocking) alongside Checkov
ci: migrate AWS OIDC config to configure-aws-credentials@v4 with tighter session policy
release: require recent green integration run and include version summary in job output
docs: update CI/CD guidance and CODEOWNERS notes for new module paths
ci_checks: pr / trivy iac (non-blocking) + SARIF upload: pass
integration / ephemeral-aws (auto-triggered by risk classifier): pass
source.review_thread: Victor Alvarez (review): Risk classifier: please ensure endpoint/DNS changes and NAT/route-table changes are both flagged. We’ve had PRs that only touch modules/endpoints but still break private DNS behavior. Also confirm integration tests still default to us-east-2 only. Sahana Iyer (author): Updated classifier to include modules/endpoints*, modules/vpc*, and any change under examples/networking*. Region remains us-east-2 only; denylist/allowlist enforced via env + SCP. Marcus Lin (review): Checkov exceptions: can you confirm we still require justification + scoped skip IDs, and that IAM boundary changes still require Security approval? Also don’t want Trivy to become blocking yet. Sahana Iyer (author): Confirmed. Checkov remains merge-blocking; Trivy is informational (continue-on-error) but uploads SARIF. CODEOWNERS updated to add modules/iam-boundaries/* under Security Eng. Connor O'Brien (review): Release gating: requiring a green integration run within 24h is good. Please document the fallback path (release-triggered integration attached to the commit) and ensure the workflow links the run URL in the release job summary. Sahana Iyer (author): Added fallback logic + job summary link to the integration run and the Terraform/provider versions used. Irene Choi (review): Workflow runtime: did the matrix consolidation actually reduce time? Sahana Iyer (author): Yes; PR checks went from ~14-18 min to ~9-11 min on typical module-only changes by avoiding repeated init and by path-filtering unchanged targets. Approvals: Victor Alvarez approved. Marcus Lin approved with note to revisit making Trivy blocking after we tune findings. Connor O'Brien approved. Merge: Squash merged by Sahana Iyer on 2025-09-10.
source.release_notes: CI/CD: PR checks now run via discovery-driven matrix for faster validation; integration tests auto-run on high-risk PRs (networking/endpoints/IAM boundary/SG egress) in addition to nightly/manual/release; security scanning keeps Checkov as blocking and adds Trivy IaC as non-blocking SARIF signal; release workflow now requires a recent green integration run and includes Terraform/provider version summary.

Approval policy unchanged in spirit: min 2 approvals; Security Eng required for IAM/logging/egress/endpoint-policy changes; CODEOWNERS paths updated to include new modules/iam-boundaries and modules/vpc-endpoints. Integration tests (ephemeral AWS): still provision/assert/destroy in CI sandbox account, but now triggered by risk classifier for high-risk PRs + nightly + manual + release. Guardrails: SCP region allowlist; deny public S3; budget alarms. Cost controls: workflow timeout; smaller footprints; prefer endpoints over NAT where possible. Troubleshooting notes updated: most common failures now are endpoint eventual consistency and IAM propagation; we added retries to specific asserts and ensured destroy runs even after assert failures. This PR intentionally does not remove Checkov or change the exception policy semantics; it adds Trivy as a non-blocking signal and consolidates workflow structure for speed and clarity.

source.title: Expose Dedicated scaling policy (min/max bounds + predictive mode) in modules and examples
source.author: Irene Choi
source.description: This PR updates the redwood-terraform modules and example configs to support the new Dedicated autoscaling policy fields (customer-configurable min/max bounds + predictive mode toggle). The provider/resource schema changes landed in the redwood monorepo; this PR focuses on surfacing the knobs in our Terraform modules, keeping backward compatibility (no behavior change unless fields are set), and documenting expected semantics + feature-flag requirements for predictive mode. Key updates: (1) Dedicated module: add optional inputs for scaling policy (min_replicas, max_replicas, scale_up_cooldown_seconds, scale_down_cooldown_seconds, predictive_mode, predictive_lead_time_seconds). (2) Examples: add a dedicated_deployment example showing cost ceiling + baseline capacity, and an example enabling predictive mode for known daily ramps. (3) Docs: README updates describing precedence (bounds clamp desired replicas), validation (min <= max), and operational notes (predictive mode requires account flag; reactive scaling still applies when forecasts are stale). (4) Outputs: expose effective scaling policy fields in outputs for visibility during plan/apply and for CI drift checks. Notes: predictive_mode is intentionally an opt-in boolean and defaults to false/null to preserve existing behavior; modules use null defaults and conditional blocks to avoid forcing zero-values into the API.
commits: modules/dedicated: add scaling_policy inputs and conditional block rendering
examples: add dedicated scaling bounds and predictive mode examples
docs: update README with semantics, validation rules, and feature flag notes
chore: run terraform fmt, update CI to lint new examples
changed_files: modules/dedicated/main.tf (render scaling_policy block only when any inputs are set)
examples/README.md (overview of new examples + when to use which)
README.md (module docs: semantics, feature flag, troubleshooting)
source.test_plan: 1) terraform fmt (repo root) 2) terraform validate for modules/dedicated and each example directory 3) plan-only run against staging workspace using a sandbox Dedicated deployment (no-op expected when scaling policy fields are unset) 4) apply in staging with min/max set (verify bound fields appear in state and API reflects values) 5) plan/apply with predictive_mode=true only in accounts with feature flag enabled (verify API accepts; otherwise expect validation error from control plane).
source.rollout_notes: No automatic customer behavior change: module defaults keep scaling policy unset unless explicitly configured. Predictive mode requires enablement via internal process (INT-9412) and is documented as such. Recommend rolling this out to pilot accounts first; include module version bump in pilot customer IaC repos before broader comms.
review_conversation: Ethan Park: Can we avoid sending zero-values for cooldowns when the user doesn’t set them? Control plane treats 0 as 'immediate', which is not what we want for defaults. Also please document that predictive_mode is gated per account.

Irene Choi (author): Updated to use null defaults + conditional block rendering so we only send fields that are set. Added README note: predictive_mode requires account flag + forecasts must be fresh; otherwise autoscaler falls back to reactive while respecting bounds.

Vanessa Ortiz: Please align naming with the control plane schema (min_replicas/max_replicas) and add an example showing a hard max as a budget ceiling. Also add a short note that max-bound saturation will show up as queueing/latency if traffic exceeds cap.

Irene Choi (author): Renamed variables to min_replicas/max_replicas (keeping old names as deprecated aliases was considered, but we never shipped them). Added examples/dedicated-scaling-bounds and a README callout about max-bound saturation impacts.

Kira Thompson: Docs read well. Can you add a small troubleshooting section pointing to the Dedicated autoscaler runbook/dashboards for 'why didn’t it scale above max?' and 'why is predictive not doing anything?'

Irene Choi (author): Added a short troubleshooting section with links and the two common cases: hitting max bound and forecasts stale/flag disabled. Also clarified that reactive signals can override prediction (within bounds).
Allison Grant: Please be explicit about which metrics customers should watch after enabling bounds/predictive. Even if the dashboards live elsewhere, list metric names at a high level.

Irene Choi (author): Added 'What to monitor' section referencing desired vs bounded replicas and bound-hit counters (names as in the observability standards doc) without adding high-cardinality labels.
Ethan Park: Approved once CI is green.
Vanessa Ortiz: LGTM.
Kira Thompson: Approved.
Allison Grant: Approved.
release_notes: Terraform modules now support configuring Dedicated autoscaling bounds (min/max replicas) and predictive scaling mode.
New examples added for baseline capacity + budget ceiling and for predictive mode ramp handling.
Defaults preserve existing behavior unless scaling policy fields are explicitly set.

source.title: Add release tagging and changelog generation
source.author: Connor O'Brien
source.release_notes: Adds a repeatable release workflow for redwood-terraform: standardized tags (vX.Y.Z), automated CHANGELOG generation, and a version bump helper for module metadata. No runtime/infra behavior changes.
source.body: Summary: This PR introduces an opinionated release workflow for redwood-terraform so we can cut alpha module versions consistently (tags + changelog + lightweight automation) without relying on ad-hoc manual steps. This is intended to support the VPC Terraform modules (alpha) program and aligns with the module versioning/release process doc. Motivation: We’ve started landing a steady stream of alpha changes (VPC, endpoints, flow logs, IAM scaffold, CI). Field teams and design partners need pinned versions and readable deltas. Today, we’re manually tagging and writing release notes, and the process is already diverging between maintainers. Goals: (1) One button / one command path to cut a release; (2) deterministic tags (vX.Y.Z); (3) generated CHANGELOG entries based on merged PR metadata; (4) minimal GitHub token scope; (5) guardrails to avoid releasing from dirty or non-main states. Non-goals: publishing to Terraform Registry (external) in alpha; changing module source layout; enforcing conventional commits across all contributors (we’ll encourage, not block). What’s included: - GitHub Actions release workflow: .github/workflows/release.yml supports workflow_dispatch with inputs (version, prerelease flag). It validates we’re on main at a clean commit, creates an annotated tag vX.Y.Z, generates/update CHANGELOG.md, and creates a GitHub Release with the same notes. - Changelog tooling: adds .cliff.toml (git-cliff config) and a scripts/release/generate_changelog.sh wrapper. We generate a scoped section for the version being released and keep CHANGELOG.md append-only. - Version bump automation: scripts/release/bump_version.sh updates module version references where we currently duplicate them (README snippet blocks + examples/* pinned module refs). This keeps examples aligned with the latest release tag. - PR labeling conventions: introduces a lightweight convention to improve changelog quality without blocking merges: labels release-note:feature|fix|security|ops|breaking (breaking allowed but requires explicit acknowledgement). The release script will still work without labels, but output will be less structured. - Safety checks: workflow refuses to run if the repo has uncommitted changes (for local scripts), if version doesn’t match semver, or if the tag already exists. Also enforces the v prefix. - Docs: adds RELEASE.md with step-by-step instructions and a "release dry run" command path for maintainers. Changed areas (high level): - .github/workflows/release.yml (new) - .github/workflows/changelog-check.yml (new; verifies CHANGELOG.md format + that it’s updated when release-note labels are present) - scripts/release/bump_version.sh (new) - scripts/release/generate_changelog.sh (new) - .cliff.toml (new) - CHANGELOG.md (seed file w/ initial alpha notes) - RELEASE.md (new) - Makefile (adds release-dry-run and changelog targets) Notes on permissions/security: The release workflow uses GitHub Actions built-in token with explicit permissions (contents: write). No PATs. The workflow does not assume access to customer accounts or infra. It only tags/releases this repo. It runs only via workflow_dispatch and is restricted to maintainers via repo settings (documented in RELEASE.md). Test plan: - Local: make changelog (verifies git-cliff runs and produces expected output); make release-dry-run (runs semver validation + changelog generation without pushing tags). - CI: ran on this PR: lint + fmt + validate + checkov + tflint all green; workflow yamllint pass. Release workflow itself validated via act-style dry run (documented) and by running workflow on a test tag in a fork. Rollout: once merged, maintainers can cut v0.1.0-alpha.1 (or v0.1.0) following RELEASE.md. We’ll use this for the next design partner drop and update the alpha release notes doc accordingly. Follow-ups: - After we land a few releases, revisit whether we want to enforce release-note labels as a required check. - Consider adding provenance / attestations (SLSA) once we start distributing beyond design partners.
commits: chore(release): add release workflow and tagging guardrails
chore(changelog): add git-cliff config + generation script
chore(versioning): add bump_version helper and update examples
docs: add RELEASE.md and maintainer checklist
ci: add changelog check workflow
source.ci_summary: CI: pass. Checks: terraform fmt/validate (pass), tflint (pass), checkov (pass), unit smoke scripts (pass), workflow lint (pass). One transient checkov flake on first run due to rate limiting; rerun succeeded.
source.review_conversation: Irene Choi (Infra) 2025-07-09: This is the right direction. Two requests before approval: (1) make sure workflow has minimal token permissions (explicitly set permissions block), and (2) add a hard guard that releases only run from main (no releasing from a random SHA/branch). Connor O'Brien 2025-07-09: Ack on both. I’ll add permissions: { contents: write } and assert github.ref == refs/heads/main. Also adding a confirmation prompt via workflow input. Sahana Iyer 2025-07-10: Please avoid introducing another toolchain dependency that’s hard to run on CI runners. Does git-cliff require extra install? Also suggest adding a make target and pinning a version. Connor O'Brien 2025-07-10: Added a pinned git-cliff action version for CI and documented local install options; make changelog now uses the containerized runner when available. Devon Price 2025-07-11: Release.yml should not write back to the repo unless the run is explicitly marked "cut release". In the current version it commits CHANGELOG.md automatically; I’d prefer the workflow creates a release from generated notes without pushing a commit, OR pushes only if approved. Connor O'Brien 2025-07-11: Updated: workflow now generates notes for the GitHub Release and (optionally) opens a PR to update CHANGELOG.md instead of committing directly to main. For alpha we default to "open PR" mode. Marcus Lin (Security) 2025-07-12: Thanks for removing PAT usage. Please confirm the workflow does not use third-party actions with broad permissions. Also add a note that release workflow is maintainer-only and should be protected by environment approvals. Connor O'Brien 2025-07-12: Confirmed. We’re using official actions + git-cliff action pinned to a SHA. Added GitHub Environment requirement to RELEASE.md; also scoped permissions and added concurrency to avoid parallel tag creation. Irene Choi 2025-07-15: Approving. The environment gating + main-only check addresses my concerns. Please coordinate first real release with me so we can sanity check tag naming (v0.x.y) aligns with the alpha versioning standard. Connor O'Brien 2025-07-15: Will do; I’ll schedule a 15-min release runthrough before cutting v0.1.0-alpha.1.
source.merge_outcome: Merged via squash into main by Connor O'Brien after approvals from Infra, CI, and Security reviewers.

source.title: Add optional VPN/DX placeholders and routing hooks (no provider assumptions)
source.author: Bruno Silva
source.release_notes: Expose stable routing outputs and optional inputs for customer-managed VPN/DX/TGW integrations without encoding provider-specific resources. Adds examples + docs for routing extension points.
source.body:
Title: Add optional VPN/DX placeholders and routing hooks (no provider assumptions)

Context
Design partners keep asking, "Where do we plug in our VPN / Direct Connect / Transit Gateway routes?" Our alpha modules intentionally avoid building VPN/DX/TGW resources (provider + enterprise topology specific), but we also need to stop forcing customers to fork the VPC module just to add a couple of routes.

This PR adds:
- Stable outputs for route table IDs and subnet groupings so customers can attach routes externally (VPN/TGW/DX) without modifying our modules.
- Optional, generic "routing hooks" inputs for adding additional routes to the private/public route tables when the target is an ID we already know (e.g., a pre-existing TGW attachment, VGW, or NAT instance) while keeping the module free of assumptions about what creates that target.
- Docs + example showing recommended patterns (customer-owned networking module creates VPN/TGW, then consumes our outputs to add routes).

Scope / non-goals
- NOT creating aws_vpn_gateway / aws_customer_gateway / aws_dx_gateway / aws_ec2_transit_gateway or attachments.
- NOT standardizing on any single enterprise connectivity topology.
- NOT attempting to manage propagation/associations for TGW route tables (customers vary widely).

Implementation details
1) New outputs in modules/vpc
- outputs for:
  - vpc_id
  - private_route_table_ids_by_az
  - public_route_table_ids_by_az
  - private_subnet_ids_by_az
  - public_subnet_ids_by_az
  - intra_subnet_ids_by_az (if enabled)
  - azs

These are intentionally shaped for common enterprise patterns (per-AZ route tables) and are stable identifiers for external modules.

2) Optional route injection (generic)
- Added variable extra_routes with schema:
  - destination_cidr (string)
  - route_table_scope ("private"|"public"|"intra")
  - target_type ("tgw"|"vgw"|"eni"|"nat_instance"|"gateway")
  - target_id (string)

We keep it as a simple switch to set the right aws_route attribute (transit_gateway_id, gateway_id, network_interface_id, etc.) without creating the target resource.

Important: We validate that only one supported target type is set per route entry and we hard-fail on "unknown" to avoid silent misroutes.

3) Example + docs
- Added examples/private-vpc-with-customer-connectivity/
  - shows a placeholder customer module creating a TGW attachment (commented/pseudocode) and then adding routes via our outputs.
  - callouts for: DNS expectations, ECR endpoints, and making sure installer can reach required services.

Why this shape?
- SE feedback (Markus/Colin): customers want a clear "attachment point" without us dictating whether it’s VPN, DX, TGW, or something else.
- Security feedback (Marcus): we should not implicitly open egress or create VPN constructs; keep connectivity owned by customer networking team.
- Ops feedback (Nina): avoid forcing route table recreation (high risk) and avoid any behavior that changes NAT strategy.

Files changed (high level)
- modules/vpc/variables.tf (extra_routes input + validation)
- modules/vpc/outputs.tf (route table + subnet outputs)
- modules/vpc/routes.tf (optional aws_route resources per scope)
- examples/private-vpc-with-customer-connectivity/main.tf
- examples/private-vpc-with-customer-connectivity/README.md
- docs/extension-points.md (new section: Customer connectivity / VPN / DX hooks)

Testing
- terraform fmt / validate
- tflint
- checkov (no new high severity findings)
- integration test harness: ephemeral VPC apply/destroy (routing hooks exercised with dummy ENI target in test)

Notes for reviewers
- I intentionally did not add any new required variables. Default behavior is unchanged.
- Outputs are additive; no renames.
- Route injection only creates routes when extra_routes is non-empty.

Review conversation (abridged)

Markus Klein (Solutions Eng): Can we make the docs explicit that this is where DX/VPN plugs in? Our pilots keep asking for "Direct Connect support" and we need to explain we support it via hooks, not by creating it.
Bruno Silva (author): Yep. Added a short section in docs/extension-points.md and an example directory. Also added wording: "customer-owned connectivity resources".

Victor Alvarez (Networking): output names should be consistent with endpoints module naming. Prefer *_ids_by_az and also provide azs output so consumers don’t have to recompute.
Bruno Silva: Updated outputs to *_ids_by_az and added azs.

Marcus Lin (Security): extra_routes target_type list is OK, but make sure we validate we don’t accidentally set multiple route target fields. Also, call out that we don’t manage route propagation.
Bruno Silva: Added input validation and README note. No propagation logic.

Title: Add optional VPN/DX placeholders and routing hooks (no provider assumptions)

Context
Design partners keep asking, "Where do we plug in our VPN / Direct Connect / Transit Gateway routes?" Our alpha modules intentionally avoid building VPN/DX/TGW resources (provider + enterprise topology specific), but we also need to stop forcing customers to fork the VPC module just to add a couple of routes.

This PR adds:
- Stable outputs for route table IDs and subnet groupings so customers can attach routes externally (VPN/TGW/DX) without modifying our modules.
- Optional, generic "routing hooks" inputs for adding additional routes to the private/public route tables when the target is an ID we already know (e.g., a pre-existing TGW attachment, VGW, or NAT instance) while keeping the module free of assumptions about what creates that target.
- Docs + example showing recommended patterns (customer-owned networking module creates VPN/TGW, then consumes our outputs to add routes).

Scope / non-goals
- NOT creating aws_vpn_gateway / aws_customer_gateway / aws_dx_gateway / aws_ec2_transit_gateway or attachments.
- NOT standardizing on any single enterprise connectivity topology.
- NOT attempting to manage propagation/associations for TGW route tables (customers vary widely).

Implementation details
1) New outputs in modules/vpc
- outputs for:
  - vpc_id
  - private_route_table_ids_by_az
  - public_route_table_ids_by_az
  - private_subnet_ids_by_az
  - public_subnet_ids_by_az
  - intra_subnet_ids_by_az (if enabled)
  - azs

These are intentionally shaped for common enterprise patterns (per-AZ route tables) and are stable identifiers for external modules.

2) Optional route injection (generic)
- Added variable extra_routes with schema:
  - destination_cidr (string)
  - route_table_scope ("private"|"public"|"intra")
  - target_type ("tgw"|"vgw"|"eni"|"nat_instance"|"gateway")
  - target_id (string)

We keep it as a simple switch to set the right aws_route attribute (transit_gateway_id, gateway_id, network_interface_id, etc.) without creating the target resource.

Important: We validate that only one supported target type is set per route entry and we hard-fail on "unknown" to avoid silent misroutes.

source.title: CI: add tflint + checkov gates; pin Terraform/AWS provider versions for reproducible builds
source.author: Sahana Iyer
source.description:
Summary
This PR hardens the `redwood-terraform` alpha module repo by adding Terraform linting + security scanning in CI and by pinning Terraform/AWS provider versions to improve reproducibility across dev machines and CI runners.

Motivation
We’ve had a few sources of drift already during dogfood: different AWS provider patch versions producing slightly different plans, and “looks fine locally” modules that fail basic best-practice checks once they hit customer environments. Since these modules are explicitly meant to encode Redwood-validated defaults, we should catch issues early and keep outputs stable.

What’s included
- Provider + Terraform version pinning:
  - Add explicit `required_version` and `required_providers` constraints across modules/examples.
  - Standardize on AWS provider `~> 5.0` with a minimum patch level validated in sandbox.
  - Add/refresh `.terraform.lock.hcl` for the example environment(s) to stabilize CI.
  - Document the compatibility matrix in the repo README (Terraform + AWS provider versions).
- TFLint:
  - Add `.tflint.hcl` with the AWS ruleset enabled.
  - Add `make lint` target + `tflint --recursive` invocation for modules + examples.
  - Configure a small allowlist of rules we’re intentionally not enforcing yet (alpha) with TODOs and owners.
- Checkov:
  - Add `make scan` target using Checkov for Terraform (IaC security baseline).
  - Configure CI to fail on HIGH/CRITICAL by default.
  - Add a minimal set of targeted suppressions for known false positives with explicit justifications (e.g., certain “encryption” checks not applicable for resources we don’t create in alpha).
- CI wiring:
  - Update GitHub Actions workflow to run: fmt -> validate -> tflint -> checkov.
  - Cache plugin downloads where possible to keep PR runtime sane.

How to run locally
- `make fmt && make validate`
- `make lint` (requires tflint; see README for install)
- `make scan` (requires checkov)

Notes on version pinning
- We’re pinning versions to reduce “plan churn” and to ensure the alpha behaves consistently for design partners.
- We’re not committing lockfiles per-module yet; only the example environments include `.terraform.lock.hcl` since they’re what CI executes end-to-end. Module consumers can choose whether to commit lockfiles.

CI issues encountered (and fixes)
- Run 1 failed: `tflint --recursive` flagged missing `required_providers` blocks in 3 modules; fixed by adding `versions.tf` files consistently.
- Run 2 failed: Checkov flagged public egress warnings on NAT Gateway route tables in the example. This is expected for the default (private subnets via NAT). Added suppressions with rationale and linked to the alpha security posture doc.
- Run 3 failed: Provider constraint mismatch between root example and module constraints (`>= 5.12.0` vs `~> 5.0`). Normalized to `>= 5.12.0, < 6.0.0` across the repo.
- Run 4 passed after updating the lockfile and ensuring CI uses the same Terraform version as documented.

Security review highlights
- Marcus requested that every Checkov suppression include:
  - A short, specific justification
  - A reference to a tracking item for removal (Linear)
  - Avoid blanket suppressions at directory level
  Addressed by scoping suppressions to resource blocks and adding TODOs.

Release impact
No functional infra behavior changes intended, but stricter CI gates may block PRs that previously passed. This is expected.

Follow-ups
- Evaluate adding `tfsec` or moving to a single consolidated IaC scanner; defer until we see signal/noise from Checkov in alpha.
- Consider enforcing provider pinning in examples only (current) vs all modules (kept constraints in modules, lockfile only in examples).

Changelog
- Added: CI lint + security scan stages (tflint, checkov)
- Added: provider + Terraform version constraints and documentation
- Fixed: inconsistent provider constraints across modules/examples

Screenshots/log excerpts
- Checkov gate example (after suppressions): HIGH/CRITICAL = 0, MEDIUM = 6 (non-blocking)
- tflint: 0 issues across modules + examples
commits: ci: add tflint action + recursive lint step
ci: add checkov scan with severity gating
terraform: normalize required_version + required_providers across modules
docs: add version compatibility + local tooling instructions
fix: update lockfile and resolve provider constraint mismatch
conversation: Irene Choi (Infra): Please pin Terraform and the AWS provider explicitly. We’ve already seen plan diffs across provider patch versions. Also: can we keep lockfiles limited to examples so we don’t force it on module consumers?
Sahana Iyer (Author): Done. Added required_version/required_providers repo-wide; lockfile committed for example env only. Updated README to call out recommended version + how to regenerate lockfile.
Victor Alvarez (Networking): tflint is now failing on modules/vpc because it can’t find required_providers in nested modules. Are we missing versions.tf in submodules?
Sahana Iyer (Author): Yep — fixed by adding versions.tf in each module directory (not just root). Re-ran CI.
Marcus Lin (Security): Checkov suppressions need to be scoped and justified. No directory-wide suppressions. Add TODO + tracking to remove where possible.
Sahana Iyer (Author): Updated suppressions to be resource-level with justification comments and linked follow-up items in ENG-4837. CI green now.
Hanae Suzuki (Security): Can we set the gate to HIGH/CRITICAL only for alpha? Medium noise seems high right now.
Sahana Iyer (Author): Agreed. Gate is HIGH/CRITICAL fail, MEDIUM informational for now; documented in docs/ci-and-validation.md.
Connor O'Brien (Release): Please ensure the CI uses the same Terraform version we’re documenting, otherwise we’ll get churn. Also add a note for maintainers on bumping provider versions safely.
Sahana Iyer (Author): Updated workflow to use the pinned Terraform version and added maintainers section in README. Thanks.
Irene Choi (Infra): Approved once CI passes on a fresh run.
CI Bot: run failed (tflint missing required_providers) -> run failed (checkov findings) -> run passed
Irene Choi (Infra): Approved.
Marcus Lin (Security): Approved with follow-up on suppressions cleanup.

source.title: Add integration test harness for ephemeral VPC (create/validate/destroy)
source.author: Sahana Iyer
source.body: Summary: This PR introduces an integration test harness that provisions an ephemeral AWS VPC using our alpha modules, validates a small set of expected invariants/outputs, and then tears everything down. The goal is to catch provider/module drift and unsafe defaults earlier than design partner pilots (e.g., subnet mapping, NAT assumptions, endpoint/DNS regressions). Motivation/Context: Unit-level terraform validate/tflint/checkov are necessary but not sufficient; we’ve already had issues that only show up once AWS resources exist (e.g., AZ assumptions, route association wiring, endpoint private DNS behavior). This harness is a minimal “smoke” environment that deploys modules the way customers will via examples, with assertions for the outputs the installer expects downstream. What’s included: (1) A Go-based Terratest suite under test/integration/ that runs terraform init/apply against examples/integration/ephemeral-vpc and performs basic assertions. (2) A dedicated example config (ephemeral-vpc) that composes modules/vpc + modules/endpoints + modules/logging with the smallest viable set of variables. (3) GitHub Actions workflow .github/workflows/integration-tests-ephemeral-vpc.yml that assumes an AWS role in the sandbox account via OIDC, runs the tests, and always attempts teardown (defer destroy). (4) Guardrails: unique name prefix per run, TTL tags on all resources, and a best-effort cleanup job to reduce orphan risk on cancelled runs. (5) Documentation in the repo README for how/when to run integration tests and the expected CI cost/time. CI/Cost/Runtime notes: Running this on every PR is expensive (AWS costs + ~18–30 min runtime depending on region/AZ capacity). Current proposal in this PR: run nightly on main + allow manual workflow_dispatch for maintainers and release candidates. We still keep fmt/validate/tflint/checkov gating per-PR (already in progress under ENG-4837 / PR-18503). Validation assertions (current set): VPC is created with expected CIDR; public/private subnets count matches inputs; private subnets have a route to NAT (when enabled); VPC endpoints created for S3/ECR API/ECR DKR/STS/CloudWatch Logs; endpoint security group is present; flow logs destination is created when enabled; key outputs are non-empty (vpc_id, private_subnet_ids, endpoint_sg_id). Notably we’re not asserting exact route table IDs or full endpoint policy JSON to avoid brittle tests; we’re targeting invariants that map to common pilot failures. Follow-ups (not in this PR): Add a second scenario for “no-NAT + endpoints-only” topology; add an assertion for private DNS settings (enabled/disabled) once we lock defaults with Security; wire a weekly budget report for sandbox account spend; consider moving from Terratest to terraform test once we’re on a baseline Terraform version across customers. Screenshots/Logs: See workflow run output for a full terraform plan/apply log; by default we redact AWS account IDs and do not print full state. Reviewer checklist: Please focus on (a) safety of the teardown paths, (b) whether assertions align with what the installer actually needs, and (c) whether the workflow scheduling is acceptable for alpha (nightly + manual).
commits: test(integration): add terratest harness for ephemeral VPC example
examples: add examples/integration/ephemeral-vpc composition config
ci: add nightly + manual GitHub Actions workflow for integration tests
docs: document integration test usage and sandbox assumptions
chore: add TTL tagging helpers + cleanup safety net
notable_snippets: Workflow scheduling: `on: { schedule: [cron: '17 3 * * *'], workflow_dispatch: {} }` and uses OIDC role assumption for the sandbox account.
Terratest teardown: `defer terraform.Destroy(t, terraformOptions)` plus a final cleanup step guarded by `if: always()` in the workflow.
TTL tags: all resources created via examples include `tags = merge(var.tags, { "redwood:ttl_hours" = "12", "redwood:owner" = "ci-integration" })`.
conversation: Victor Alvarez (review): Nice. Two questions: 1) Are we comfortable with Terratest here vs terraform test? 2) Can we avoid validating route table IDs directly (brittle across AWS)?

title: Add integration test harness for ephemeral VPC (create/validate/destroy)
preview: Summary: This PR introduces an integration test harness that provisions an ephemeral AWS VPC using our alpha modules, validates a small set of expected invariants/outputs, and then tears everything down. The goal is to catch provider/module drift and unsafe defaults earlier than design partner pilots (e.g., subnet mapping, NAT assumptions, endpoint/DNS regressions). Motivation/Context: Unit-level terraform validate/tflint/checkov are necessary but not sufficient; we’ve already had issues that only show up once AWS resources exist (e.g., AZ assumptions, route association wiring, endpoint private DNS behavior). This harness is a minimal “smoke” environment that deploys modules the way customers will via examples, with assertions for the outputs the installer expects downstream. What’s included: (1) A Go-based Terratest suite under test/integration/ that runs terraform init/apply against examples/integration/ephemeral-vpc and performs basic assertions. (2) A dedicated example config (ephemeral-vpc) that composes modules/vpc + modules/endpoints + modules/logging with the smallest viable set of variables. (3) GitHub Actions workflow .github/workflows/integration-tests-ephemeral-vpc.yml that assumes an AWS role in the sandbox account via OIDC, runs the tests, and always attempts teardown (defer destroy). (4) Guardrails: unique name prefix per run, TTL tags on all resources, and a best-effort cleanup job to reduce orphan risk on cancelled runs. (5) Documentation in the repo README for how/when to run integration tests and the expected CI cost/time. CI/Cost/Runtime notes: Running this on every PR is expensive (AWS costs + ~18–30 min runtime depending on region/AZ capacity). Current proposal in this PR: run nightly on main + allow manual workflow_dispatch for maintainers and release candidates. We still keep fmt/validate/tflint/checkov gating per-PR (already in progress under ENG-4837 / PR-18503). Validation assertions (current set): VPC is created with expected CIDR; public/private subnets count matches inputs; private subnets have a route to NAT (when enabled); VPC endpoints created for S3/ECR API/ECR DKR/STS/CloudWatch Logs; endpoint security group is present; flow logs destination is created when enabled; key outputs are non-empty (vpc_id, private_subnet_ids, endpoint_sg_id). Notably we’re not asserting exact route table IDs or full endpoint policy JSON to avoid brittle tests; we’re targeting invariants that map to common pilot failures. Follow-ups (not in this PR): Add a second scenario for “no-NAT + endpoints-only” topology; add an assertion for private DNS settings (enabled/disabled) once we lock defaults with Security; wire a weekly budget report for sandbox account spend; consider moving from Terratest to terraform test once we’re on a baseline Terraform version across customers. Screenshots/Logs: See workflow run output for a full terraform plan/apply log; by default we redact AWS account IDs and do not print full state. Reviewer checklist: Please focus on (a) safety of the teardown paths, (b) whether assertions align with what the installer actually needs, and (c) whether the workflow scheduling is acceptable for alpha (nightly + manual).

source.title: Add VPC Flow Logs defaults + configurable log destinations (CloudWatch/S3)
source.author: Bruno Silva
source.body:
Title: Add VPC Flow Logs defaults + configurable log destinations (CloudWatch/S3)

Context
We’ve had repeated pilot friction around “are we capturing network telemetry by default, and where does it go?” For Redwood Private VPC deployments we want sane, audit-friendly defaults while still letting customers route logs to their own sinks (CloudWatch or S3 today; SIEM forwarding remains out-of-scope for these modules).

This PR introduces a new flow logs submodule and wires it into the example envs:
- Default-on VPC Flow Logs for the created VPC
- Destination options: CloudWatch Logs or S3 (customer-owned bucket supported)
- Explicit retention/lifecycle inputs with conservative defaults and clear cost/audit trade-offs

What changed
1) New module: modules/flow-logs
- Creates aws_flow_log for the VPC
- Supports traffic_type = ALL by default (input override allowed)
- Destination selection:
  - cloudwatch: creates/uses a CloudWatch Log Group + IAM role for vpc-flow-logs.amazonaws.com
  - s3: writes to S3 bucket ARN (either module-managed or customer-provided)
- Default format uses the AWS recommended extended fields (includes: account-id, vpc-id, subnet-id, instance-id, interface-id, srcaddr/dstaddr, srcport/dstport, protocol, tcp-flags, packets/bytes, start/end, action, log-status)

2) Retention/lifecycle defaults + inputs
- CloudWatch retention default set to 90 days (var.flow_logs_cloudwatch_retention_days) to meet common audit expectations; customers can increase/decrease.
- S3 lifecycle default set to 365 days (var.flow_logs_s3_expiration_days) when using module-managed bucket; if customer supplies their own bucket, lifecycle is not mutated.
- Both destinations support optional KMS encryption:
  - CloudWatch: kms_key_id on the log group
  - S3: bucket SSE-KMS when module manages the bucket; customer buckets are assumed to be already compliant

3) Example updates
- examples/alpha/private-vpc now enables flow logs by default and demonstrates both destination types via commented tfvars blocks.

4) Docs
- README updates for flow logs behavior, destination trade-offs, and “customer-owned bucket” expectations (encryption, access logs, lifecycle handled by customer).

Key implementation notes
- We deliberately do not create CloudWatch subscription filters / SIEM forwarding here. The module outputs log_group_name/log_group_arn (CloudWatch) or bucket_arn/prefix (S3) so customers can attach their own forwarding pipelines.
- For S3 destination we include an optional log_prefix to keep multi-env buckets tidy.
- For CloudWatch destination we create a least-privilege IAM role allowing only logs:CreateLogStream and logs:PutLogEvents to the specific log group.

Illustrative snippet (module interface)
- enable_flow_logs (bool, default true)
- flow_logs_destination (string: "cloudwatch"|"s3", default "cloudwatch")
- flow_logs_cloudwatch_retention_days (number, default 90)
- flow_logs_s3_bucket_arn (string, optional; if set, module will not create bucket)
- flow_logs_s3_expiration_days (number, default 365; only applies to module-managed bucket)
- flow_logs_kms_key_id (string, optional)

Review discussion highlights
- Kevin Osei (Security): requested we avoid silently writing logs into a Redwood-managed bucket by default; agreed. Default destination remains CloudWatch (customer account) and any S3 bucket is explicitly customer-owned unless they opt into module-managed bucket creation.
- Allison Grant (Observability): asked that we use traffic_type=ALL by default and include log-status/action fields to ease troubleshooting. Incorporated.
- Marcus Lin (Security Eng): requested KMS hooks for both CloudWatch and S3 paths and a clear doc note that customer-owned buckets must enforce encryption + least-privilege bucket policy. Added.
- Irene Choi (Infra): raised cost concerns about CloudWatch retention defaults. We kept default at 90 days to align with typical audit requirements for regulated pilots; customers can set lower values, and docs call out expected cost drivers.

CI
- terraform fmt/validate: pass
- tflint: pass
- checkov: pass (initial failure on S3 bucket public access block in the module-managed bucket path; fixed by adding PublicAccessBlock + explicit bucket ownership controls)
- integration tests: skipped (not yet wired for this module in ENG-4860 harness; follow-up ticket to extend ephemeral VPC test to assert flow log resource creation)

Files/areas changed (high level)
- modules/flow-logs/* (new)
- modules/vpc/outputs.tf (expose vpc_id for flow logs wiring in examples)
- examples/alpha/private-vpc/* (enable + tfvars documentation)
- docs/README.md (new section: Flow Logs)

Merge
- Merged via squash to main.

Follow-ups
- ENG-4860: extend ephemeral integration test to assert flow logs creation and destination-specific outputs
- Add optional CloudWatch log group name override (if customers want to centralize logs) after first design partner feedback

source.title: Add IAM scaffold for Redwood Private installer execution role (with permission boundaries)
source.author: Bruno Silva
source.release_notes: Adds an optional IAM scaffold module for Redwood Private installer execution: creates (or references) an execution role, attaches a least-privilege policy, and supports customer-enforced permission boundaries by default.
source.body:
## Summary
This PR introduces an alpha IAM scaffolding module to support running `redwood-private-installer` in customer AWS accounts using a dedicated execution role. The module creates an IAM role + inline policy (or wires to an existing role), and is designed to be compatible with customer permission boundary requirements from day one.

The goal is not to encode every enterprise IAM topology, but to provide a Redwood-validated default that works for most pilots and gives security teams a clear review surface.

## Why
During discovery/pilots we repeatedly hit delays around: (1) "what IAM does the installer need?" and (2) "how do we enforce least privilege and boundaries?". Providing a standardized scaffold reduces iteration time and makes the rest of the VPC module suite easier to adopt.

## What changed
- New module: `modules/iam_installer_execution_role`
  - Creates `aws_iam_role.installer_execution` with configurable trust policy
  - Attaches an inline policy document generated via `aws_iam_policy_document` (split into statements for readability and checkov hygiene)
  - Optional permission boundary support (`permission_boundary_arn` input)
  - Outputs: `installer_execution_role_arn`, `installer_execution_role_name`, `installer_execution_role_unique_id`, `installer_execution_policy_json`
- New example wiring in `examples/private_vpc_alpha` to demonstrate expected usage and naming.
- Documentation:
  - Adds README section describing recommended boundary model and how customers can supply an existing pre-approved role.

## Inputs (high level)
- `create_role` (bool): default true
- `existing_role_arn` (string): if provided and `create_role=false`, module only validates/outputs
- `permission_boundary_arn` (string): optional but strongly recommended for enterprise pilots
- `trusted_principals` (list): AWS principals allowed to assume role (defaults to the account root; can be narrowed)
- `tags` (map): required by our internal standards and makes audit review easier

## Policy scope (alpha)
The inline policy is intentionally conservative and focused on installer needs for EKS + supporting AWS primitives:
- EC2/VPC describe and limited mutate operations required for cluster/security group wiring
- EKS create/update/describe as needed by installer
- CloudWatch Logs (create log group/stream + put events) for installer-run logs (optional, can be disabled)
- IAM `PassRole` restricted to roles created by the installer (conditioned by path + boundary requirement) to reduce privilege escalation risk

> Note: We expect to tighten/adjust based on installer preflight checks and real pilot behavior. The policy is generated from structured documents so future diffs are reviewable.

## Security posture / requested changes addressed
Requested by Security Eng (Marcus/Hanae) and incorporated here:
- Permission boundary support is first-class (role is created with boundary if provided).
- Trust policy is configurable; docs recommend narrowing to a specific CI principal / human breakglass role.
- Reduced wildcard usage and added conditions where feasible to satisfy checkov and internal review standards.
- Set `max_session_duration` to 1h by default.
- Added explicit tagging on IAM resources.

## Validation
- `terraform fmt` / `terraform validate`
- `tflint` (aws ruleset)
- `checkov`
- Manual plan against sandbox account (no apply) to validate policy renders and role attaches correctly.

## Follow-ups
- Installer team: align preflight checks with these permissions and document any required deltas (tracked under ENG-4869).
- Docs: add a "recommended boundary policy snippet" customers can reuse (separate PR).

## Screenshots / snippets
Example output policy excerpt (trimmed):
- Allow `eks:*` on cluster resources under the configured name prefix
- Allow `iam:PassRole` only when the target role has the configured permission boundary

No breaking changes; module is additive and optional.
commits: Add iam_installer_execution_role module skeleton and README
Generate installer execution policy via aws_iam_policy_document (split statements)
Add permission boundary support + max session duration default
Tighten PassRole conditions and trust policy inputs per security review
Wire example usage in private_vpc_alpha and add outputs
review_conversation: Marcus Lin (Security): The initial inline policy still has broad iam:PassRole. We need a boundary-based condition or a hard restriction to installer-created roles, otherwise it’s too easy to escalate. Also please default max session duration to 1h and require tags.

Bruno Silva (Author): Agreed. Updated PassRole to require the target role to have the configured permission boundary (when boundary is set) and restricted resources to roles under /redwood-private-installer/. Added max_session_duration=3600 and made tags required in module interface.

Hanae Suzuki (Security): Trust policy defaulting to account root is fine for alpha, but we should document the recommended narrowing (e.g., CI role or breakglass). Also checkov is flagging wildcard actions in the EC2 statements — can we split describe vs mutate and scope mutate to resource ARNs where possible?

Bruno Silva (Author): Added docs on narrowing trusted_principals and split EC2 describe actions into a separate statement. Mutate actions are now scoped to SGs/ENIs with a tag condition where AWS supports it; kept describes broad because AWS doesn’t support resource-level for many of them.
Irene Choi (Infra): Please make sure outputs are stable and named consistently with the other modules (…_arn, …_name). Also suggest outputting the rendered policy JSON for audit reviews.
Bruno Silva (Author): Done — outputs renamed to installer_execution_role_arn/name/unique_id and added installer_execution_policy_json output.
Victor Alvarez (Networking): Example wiring looks good. One nit: module README should call out that this role is for the installer (cluster/app) and not required for the VPC module apply itself.
Bruno Silva (Author): Added explicit note in README and in examples/private_vpc_alpha comments.

Naomi Feldman (Security/Compliance PM): Please ensure we’re not implying this satisfies all enterprise controls; include alpha language and note that customers may need to layer their own boundary policy + approvals.
Bruno Silva (Author): Added alpha disclaimers + guidance to supply existing pre-approved role when required.
Marcus Lin (Security): Re-reviewed latest. PassRole restriction + boundary conditioning looks acceptable for alpha. Approved once CI green.
CI Bot: checkov failed (CKV_AWS_109) - IAM policy statement allows * resource without condition.
Bruno Silva (Author): Fixed by adding tag-based condition for security group mutations and scoping remaining * resources to read-only describe statements. Re-ran CI.
CI Bot: All checks passed.
Marcus Lin (Security): Approved.
Hanae Suzuki (Security): Approved.
Irene Choi (Infra): Approved. Merge when ready.
