  - Attaches an inline policy document generated via `aws_iam_policy_document` (split into statements for readability and checkov hygiene)
  - Optional permission boundary support (`permission_boundary_arn` input)
  - Outputs: `installer_execution_role_arn`, `installer_execution_role_name`, `installer_execution_role_unique_id`, `installer_execution_policy_json`
- New example wiring in `examples/private_vpc_alpha` to demonstrate expected usage and naming.
- Documentation:
  - Adds README section describing recommended boundary model and how customers can supply an existing pre-approved role.

## Inputs (high level)
- `create_role` (bool): default true
- `existing_role_arn` (string): if provided and `create_role=false`, module only validates/outputs
- `permission_boundary_arn` (string): optional but strongly recommended for enterprise pilots
- `trusted_principals` (list): AWS principals allowed to assume role (defaults to the account root; can be narrowed)
- `tags` (map): required by our internal standards and makes audit review easier

## Policy scope (alpha)
The inline policy is intentionally conservative and focused on installer needs for EKS + supporting AWS primitives:
- EC2/VPC describe and limited mutate operations required for cluster/security group wiring
- EKS create/update/describe as needed by installer
- CloudWatch Logs (create log group/stream + put events) for installer-run logs (optional, can be disabled)
- IAM `PassRole` restricted to roles created by the installer (conditioned by path + boundary requirement) to reduce privilege escalation risk

> Note: We expect to tighten/adjust based on installer preflight checks and real pilot behavior. The policy is generated from structured documents so future diffs are reviewable.

## Security posture / requested changes addressed
Requested by Security Eng (Marcus/Hanae) and incorporated here:
- Permission boundary support is first-class (role is created with boundary if provided).
- Trust policy is configurable; docs recommend narrowing to a specific CI principal / human breakglass role.
- Reduced wildcard usage and added conditions where feasible to satisfy checkov and internal review standards.
- Set `max_session_duration` to 1h by default.
- Added explicit tagging on IAM resources.

## Validation
- `terraform fmt` / `terraform validate`
- `tflint` (aws ruleset)
- `checkov`
- Manual plan against sandbox account (no apply) to validate policy renders and role attaches correctly.

## Follow-ups
- Installer team: align preflight checks with these permissions and document any required deltas (tracked under ENG-4869).
- Docs: add a "recommended boundary policy snippet" customers can reuse (separate PR).

## Screenshots / snippets
Example output policy excerpt (trimmed):
- Allow `eks:*` on cluster resources under the configured name prefix
- Allow `iam:PassRole` only when the target role has the configured permission boundary

No breaking changes; module is additive and optional.

source.title: Add VPC module with subnet + routing defaults
source.author: Victor Alvarez
commits: vpc: initial module scaffold + variable interface
vpc: create public/private subnets per AZ with opinionated CIDR splitting
vpc: route tables + IGW routes for public subnets
vpc: NAT gateway strategy (per-AZ default, single-NAT optional)
vpc: add EKS subnet tags + default resource tags
examples: add private-eks-vpc example env
docs: add design notes + constraints section
ci: wire terraform fmt/validate on examples + modules
source.body: Summary: This PR implements the core `modules/vpc` module for the Private VPC Terraform modules (alpha). The goal is to provide a Redwood-validated default VPC layout suitable for Redwood Private deployments that run on EKS (assumed) with private worker nodes and conservative routing defaults. This module focuses on: VPC creation, public/private subnet layout across AZs, route tables, and a NAT strategy with explicit trade-offs documented. Motivation: Technical discovery/pilots keep getting blocked on basic networking variability (subnet layouts, route tables, NAT/egress assumptions). We want a default that works for most enterprise VPC baselines while remaining composable with future modules (endpoints/logging/iam) and customer-specific connectivity (VPN/DX) without forcing premature complexity. What’s included: (1) VPC with DNS enabled by default: `enable_dns_support=true` and `enable_dns_hostnames=true`. (2) Subnet layout: public + private subnets per AZ (min 2 AZs recommended). CIDR splitting is opinionated but overridable via explicit subnet CIDRs. (3) Routing: public subnets route `0.0.0.0/0` to an Internet Gateway; private subnets route `0.0.0.0/0` to NAT Gateway(s). (4) NAT strategy defaults: default is NAT Gateway per AZ (`one_nat_gateway_per_az=true`) to avoid cross-AZ dependency and improve fault isolation; optional `single_nat_gateway=true` for cost-sensitive sandboxes/dev. (5) Tags for EKS compatibility: default EKS subnet role tags and cluster discovery tags are supported. By default we tag public subnets with `kubernetes.io/role/elb=1` and private subnets with `kubernetes.io/role/internal-elb=1`. We also support `kubernetes.io/cluster/<cluster_name>=shared` when `eks_cluster_name` is provided (alpha assumes cluster created elsewhere; this module only prepares network). (6) Example environment: `examples/private-eks-vpc` shows minimal inputs, expected outputs, and common overrides for enterprise CIDR requirements. Public interface (high level): Required inputs: `name`, `vpc_cidr`, `azs` (list). Common inputs: `public_subnet_cidrs`, `private_subnet_cidrs` (optional, otherwise calculated), `single_nat_gateway` (bool), `one_nat_gateway_per_az` (bool), `eks_cluster_name` (optional), `tags` (map). Outputs: `vpc_id`, `public_subnet_ids`, `private_subnet_ids`, `public_route_table_ids`, `private_route_table_ids`, `nat_gateway_ids`, `igw_id`, `azs`. Design notes / constraints (important for Redwood Private + EKS): - This module does NOT create VPC Endpoints yet. For truly “no public egress” environments, you will need endpoints from the follow-up endpoints module (tracked separately). For now, NAT provides outbound access for pulling images, talking to AWS APIs, etc. - EKS private endpoint: EKS API endpoint access mode is not set here; however, for Private deployments we assume customers will configure private endpoint access and ensure VPC DNS/resolver behavior is stable. We explicitly enable VPC DNS support/hostnames to reduce the most common private endpoint failure mode. - Ingress/egress policy: This module deliberately does not create security groups. SG patterns are split into a separate module/PR so we can iterate on least-privilege without blocking VPC composition. - AZ assumptions: the module behaves best with >=2 AZs. If you pass 1 AZ, NAT-per-AZ and CIDR calculations still work, but we consider it out of scope for “production-like” alpha deployments (documented in README). - IPv6: not included in alpha. - Isolated/data subnets: not included yet; can be added later as an extension (DB/cache patterns) without breaking the current contract. Illustrative snippet (from README): `module "vpc" { source = "../../modules/vpc" name = "acme-redwood" vpc_cidr = "10.20.0.0/16" azs = ["us-east-1a", "us-east-1b", "us-east-1c"] one_nat_gateway_per_az = true eks_cluster_name = "acme-redwood-eks" tags = { "owner" = "platform" "environment" = "poc" } }` Implementation details: - Subnet CIDR computation uses deterministic splitting by AZ index when explicit CIDRs are not provided, and validates that the number of computed CIDRs matches the AZ count. - NAT logic: when `one_nat_gateway_per_az=true` we create one EIP + NAT GW per public subnet/AZ and route each private route table to the local NAT. When `single_nat_gateway=true` we create one NAT in the first public subnet and route all private subnets to it (documented as reduced fault tolerance). Inputs are validated to prevent conflicting flags (e.g., both single NAT and per-AZ). - Tagging: all resources get `Name` plus common tags; subnets optionally receive EKS discovery tags. CI: - GitHub Actions runs `terraform fmt -check` and `terraform validate` for module + example. CI checks: `terraform-validate (ubuntu-latest) ... PASS`, `fmt ... PASS`. Review conversation highlights: - Irene Choi (Infra): requested we default to per-AZ NAT and document the cost vs reliability trade-off; also asked for explicit outputs for route table IDs to support downstream modules. Addressed in commits 4 and 5; added outputs + README section “NAT strategy”. - Nina Petrova (Private Deployments): asked to call out EKS private control plane constraints and ensure VPC DNS settings are enabled by default. Added `docs/architecture/vpc-module-design-notes.md` and README “EKS constraints” section; confirmed DNS flags in main. - Marcus Lin (Security Eng): flagged that NAT-by-default implies outbound egress and asked that we be explicit that endpoints module will be the “secure-by-default no-public-egress” path. Added README warning and a TODO list that points to endpoints module follow-up. - Hanae Suzuki (Security templates): requested we avoid creating any overly permissive SGs in the VPC module. Confirmed scope: no SGs in this PR; SG patterns are separate. - Simran Kaur (Ref arch review): asked for a diagram in docs and a clearer mapping between outputs and the installer needs. Added a short “Integration points” section listing typical consumers (installer, endpoints module, logging module). Checklist: [x] Module README includes required inputs/outputs and constraints [x] Example `private-eks-vpc` runs `terraform validate` [x] CI green [x] Notes added for extension points (endpoints, logging, connectivity hooks) Merge: Squash merged into `main` on 2025-06-09. Follow-ups: - Add endpoints module (ECR/S3/STS/CloudWatch Logs/etc.) and update example to support no-internet-egress deployments. - Add security group patterns module and align with installer preflight expectations.

source.title: Add VPC endpoints module + private DNS options for Redwood Private (alpha)
source.author: Bruno Silva
release_notes: New `modules/vpc-endpoints` for Gateway + Interface endpoints used by Redwood Private on AWS.
Adds private DNS controls to avoid breaking customer DNS/resolvers while still enabling endpoint usage.
Example environment now provisions endpoints + security group for endpoint ENIs.
source.body:
## Summary
This adds an opinionated `vpc-endpoints` Terraform module for the Redwood Private VPC footprint (alpha). The module creates a baseline set of Gateway + Interface endpoints commonly required in fully-private subnets (no public egress) and exposes explicit inputs for private DNS behavior.

Primary motivation: reduce pilot failures related to image pulls / registry access / AWS API access in private subnets by making endpoints part of our reference modules, while staying compatible with customer DNS strategies (Route 53 Resolver, custom forwarders, split-horizon, etc.).

## What’s included
### New module: `modules/vpc-endpoints`
Creates endpoints in the provided VPC + subnets:
- **Gateway endpoint**: S3 (`com.amazonaws.<region>.s3`) with route table association(s)
- **Interface endpoints** (baseline):
  - ECR API (`com.amazonaws.<region>.ecr.api`)
  - ECR DKR (`com.amazonaws.<region>.ecr.dkr`)
  - STS (`com.amazonaws.<region>.sts`)
  - CloudWatch Logs (`com.amazonaws.<region>.logs`)
  - CloudWatch Monitoring (`com.amazonaws.<region>.monitoring`)
  - EC2 (`com.amazonaws.<region>.ec2`) (needed for some node lifecycle flows depending on cluster config)

Also provides a shared security group for endpoint ENIs (default: allow 443 from private subnet CIDRs + any additional CIDRs passed via inputs).

### Private DNS controls
We’ve had multiple pilot/customer environments where enabling Private DNS on interface endpoints causes unexpected resolution changes due to existing Route 53 Resolver rules / custom DNS forwarders.

Inputs:
- `interface_private_dns_enabled` (bool, default **true**): global default for interface endpoints
- `interface_private_dns_overrides` (map(string)=bool): per-service override keyed by short name (e.g. `ecr_api`, `ecr_dkr`, `sts`, `logs`, `monitoring`, `ec2`)
- `enable_*` toggles per endpoint (e.g. `enable_ecr`, `enable_sts`, `enable_logs`) to support environments that have a subset already provisioned by central networking teams

Behavior:
- If an endpoint is enabled and its private DNS is enabled, we set `private_dns_enabled = true` for that endpoint.
- If disabled, we still provision the endpoint but do not flip private DNS; customers can manage DNS explicitly (and the installer can use endpoint-specific DNS names if needed).

### Outputs
- `gateway_endpoint_id_s3`
- `interface_endpoint_ids` (map)
- `interface_endpoint_dns_entries` (map of lists; useful for troubleshooting)
- `endpoints_security_group_id`

## Changes in examples
- `examples/alpha/private-vpc/` now wires in the endpoints module and passes the private subnets + route tables.
- Added a short README section explaining the trade-offs of private DNS and how to debug resolution (dig against VPC resolver, check resolver rules, confirm endpoint private hosted zone association).

## CI updates
- GitHub Actions now runs `terraform fmt -check` and `terraform validate` for:
  - `modules/vpc-endpoints`
  - `examples/alpha/private-vpc` (init with `-backend=false`)
- This is intentionally lightweight; integration tests come later in ENG-4860.

## Notes / constraints
- This module assumes AWS provider >= 5.x and Terraform >= 1.5 (consistent with repo baseline).
- Subnets passed to interface endpoints should be private subnets with appropriate NACLs allowing 443 to the endpoint ENIs.

## Summary

Primary motivation: reduce pilot failures related to image pulls / registry access / AWS API access in private subnets by making endpoints part of our reference modules, while staying compatible with customer DNS strategies (Route 53 Resolver, custom forwarders, split-horizon, etc.).

## What’s included
### New module: `modules/vpc-endpoints`
Creates endpoints in the provided VPC + subnets:
- **Gateway endpoint**: S3 (`com.amazonaws.<region>.s3`) with route table association(s)
- **Interface endpoints** (baseline):
  - ECR API (`com.amazonaws.<region>.ecr.api`)
  - ECR DKR (`com.amazonaws.<region>.ecr.dkr`)
  - STS (`com.amazonaws.<region>.sts`)
  - CloudWatch Logs (`com.amazonaws.<region>.logs`)
  - CloudWatch Monitoring (`com.amazonaws.<region>.monitoring`)
  - EC2 (`com.amazonaws.<region>.ec2`) (needed for some node lifecycle flows depending on cluster config)

Also provides a shared security group for endpoint ENIs (default: allow 443 from private subnet CIDRs + any additional CIDRs passed via inputs).

### Private DNS controls
We’ve had multiple pilot/customer environments where enabling Private DNS on interface endpoints causes unexpected resolution changes due to existing Route 53 Resolver rules / custom DNS forwarders.

Inputs:
- `interface_private_dns_enabled` (bool, default **true**): global default for interface endpoints
- `interface_private_dns_overrides` (map(string)=bool): per-service override keyed by short name (e.g. `ecr_api`, `ecr_dkr`, `sts`, `logs`, `monitoring`, `ec2`)
- `enable_*` toggles per endpoint (e.g. `enable_ecr`, `enable_sts`, `enable_logs`) to support environments that have a subset already provisioned by central networking teams

- If an endpoint is enabled and its private DNS is enabled, we set `private_dns_enabled = true` for that endpoint.
- If disabled, we still provision the endpoint but do not flip private DNS; customers can manage DNS explicitly (and the installer can use endpoint-specific DNS names if needed).

- `gateway_endpoint_id_s3`
- `interface_endpoint_ids` (map)
- `interface_endpoint_dns_entries` (map of lists; useful for troubleshooting)
- `endpoints_security_group_id`

  - CloudWatch Logs PutLogEvents succeeds from private subnet

source.title: Add security group patterns for private ingress/egress (EKS + installer-friendly outputs)
source.author: Bruno Silva
source.description: This PR introduces an opinionated set of Security Group (SG) modules/patterns for Redwood Private AWS deployments (alpha). The intent is to reduce pilot variability and common discovery churn by shipping Redwood-validated defaults for: (1) EKS worker/node SG behavior (private nodes), (2) load balancer SGs for internal ingress (ALB/NLB) and controlled cross-subnet traffic, and (3) egress policy that works in a mostly-private VPC with VPC endpoints (ECR/S3/STS/CloudWatch Logs, etc.). Key goals: keep the default posture secure (no accidental broad public egress), but avoid breaking real-world installs where customers still need proxy/NAT-based egress or have non-standard endpoint/DNS setups. The modules are designed to be composable and to expose controlled extension points (additional egress CIDRs, additional destination SGs, and an explicit opt-in for “wide egress”). Summary of changes: Added new module `modules/security-groups` with submodules `eks_nodes`, `internal_lb`, and `egress`. Added a shared `rules` helper to centralize common TCP/UDP rules and keep diffs readable. Default node SG: ingress from cluster SG (EKS-managed), ingress from internal LB SG on app ports (configurable list, default 443), and egress constrained to VPC endpoints (via prefix lists and endpoint ENI SGs) + DNS + NTP (optional). Default internal LB SG: ingress from allowlisted CIDRs / SGs (supports customer VPN/DX CIDRs) and egress only to node SG on configured target ports. Default egress SG pattern: supports two modes: (a) “endpoint-only” (default) where 443 egress is restricted to endpoint prefix lists and/or endpoint SG IDs, and (b) “wide” where 0.0.0.0/0 egress on 443 is allowed for customers who require NAT/proxy access during bring-up (explicit input `enable_wide_egress = true`, default false). Outputs: `node_security_group_id`, `internal_lb_security_group_id`, `egress_security_group_id`, plus lists for per-rule debugging to make installer preflight messages actionable. Example usage added to `examples/alpha/private-eks-vpc/` and referenced in README. Notes: This PR does not attempt to model every enterprise firewall topology; instead, it standardizes the baseline and provides extension points. In particular, customers with explicit proxy egress can attach the egress SG to proxy ENIs or override via `additional_egress_*` inputs. Checklist: [x] Terraform fmt/validate; [x] tflint; [x] checkov (with documented suppressions where appropriate); [x] example plan applies in sandbox; [x] docs updated for supported extension points; [x] aligns with installer expectations for private image pulls (ECR/S3/STSv2).
commits: feat(security-groups): add base SG rule helpers and naming conventions
feat(modules/security-groups): add eks_nodes SG pattern with controlled ingress + endpoint-first egress
feat(modules/security-groups): add internal_lb SG pattern for private ALB/NLB to node targets
feat(modules/security-groups): add egress SG pattern with endpoint-only default + explicit wide-egress opt-in
docs(examples): wire SG module into alpha private EKS example and update README inputs/outputs
chore(ci): tighten checkov config and add tests for SG rule generation
ci_checks: checkov: pass (2 suppressions added with justification)
source.conversation: Victor Alvarez (review): The module shape looks good, but we need to be careful about the semantic meaning of “egress SG” in AWS. If customers attach multiple SGs to nodes, AWS unions the rules—so the secure default can be defeated by attaching a permissive SG elsewhere. Can we make the default still safe while not pretending it’s enforced? Also, can we prefer endpoint ENI SG IDs over prefix lists where possible? Bruno Silva (author): Agree. I added language to the module README: ‘This module provides a secure baseline but cannot prevent additional SG attachments.’ For endpoints, the module now supports both `endpoint_prefix_list_ids` and `endpoint_security_group_ids` and encourages endpoint SGs when customers own them. Marcus Lin (security review): Strong request: default must not allow 0.0.0.0/0 egress. We’ve seen pilots where “temporary” wide egress becomes permanent. If we need a break-glass option, it must be explicit and loud. Also, do not allow all VPC CIDR by default unless justified. Bruno Silva (author): Updated defaults: `enable_wide_egress` is false and guarded; when true we tag the SG with `redwood.io/wide-egress=true` and print a warning in README + example. Removed default allow-to-vpc-cidr egress; it’s now `additional_egress_cidrs` (empty by default). Nina Petrova (private deployments): For EKS private control plane, ensure nodes can reach the cluster endpoint and CoreDNS resolution works consistently. Some customers use VPC resolver endpoints; others rely on the default AmazonProvidedDNS. We should avoid overfitting. Bruno Silva (author): Node SG egress includes UDP/TCP 53 to the VPC resolver address range via `dns_egress_cidrs` input (defaults to the VPC CIDR, with clear guidance to set it to resolver endpoint IPs when used). Control plane connectivity remains governed by EKS-managed cluster SG rules; our module expects `cluster_security_group_id` as input and adds required ingress from that SG. Irene Choi (infra): The initial version had separate SGs per endpoint. That’s too many moving pieces for alpha. Please keep it to three SGs max and make the extension points straightforward. Bruno Silva (author): Simplified: three SGs as described (nodes/internal-lb/egress). Endpoints are referenced as destinations rather than modeled as their own SGs (optional input if customer provides endpoint SG IDs). Sean Gallagher (SRE): Concern about operability: in practice, debugging broken installs is easier when there’s a quick ‘make it work’ path. Can we support a temporary egress mode without rewriting code? Bruno Silva (author): Added `enable_wide_egress` + `wide_egress_ports` (default [443]) and documented a recommended rollback plan: start endpoint-only, only enable wide egress for initial bring-up if required, then revert once endpoints/proxy are validated. Hanae Suzuki (security templates): checkov is flagging ingress rules that depend on user-provided CIDRs. That’s fine, but we need strong validation and examples. Bruno Silva (author): Added variable validation: CIDRs must be valid and non-empty; added example showing allowlisting a VPN CIDR and a customer bastion SG; added checkov suppressions only for the module that intentionally supports customer-provided allowlists, with justification text. Final outcome: approvals from Victor + Marcus after tightening defaults and documenting the break-glass path; merged after CI green.
source.release_notes: Alpha: Added opinionated security group modules for Redwood Private AWS VPC deployments (EKS nodes, internal load balancers, and endpoint-first egress). Default posture avoids broad public egress; provides explicit opt-in for temporary wide egress when required during bring-up.

source.title: Private: add version pinning, upgrade window controls, and artifact source configuration
source.author: Bruno Silva
release_notes: Private Terraform modules now support pinning Redwood Private platform versions for safer upgrades.
Adds optional maintenance/upgrade window variables to discourage accidental daytime upgrades and provide consistent operator intent signals.
Adds artifact source configuration inputs (online) as a precursor to offline/mirror support.
source.body:
## Summary
This PR adds explicit Terraform inputs for Redwood Private lifecycle management: (1) version pinning (N-1 → N upgrade default), (2) upgrade window configuration to encode operator intent and reduce accidental upgrades outside of maintenance windows, and (3) artifact source configuration to make artifact provenance explicit (registry/chart repo URLs) and unblock future offline mirror work.

These changes are intentionally additive and default to today’s behavior when unset.

## Motivation
We’re rolling out a safer, more deterministic Private upgrade workflow (plan + prechecks + backup/rollback). Terraform is a primary entry point for VPC Private installs, and we need a consistent way to:
- Pin the target Redwood Private platform version across environments
- Avoid accidental upgrades by making a maintenance window an explicit input (and validated when provided)
- Standardize where artifacts come from so the installer can validate availability and we can later support mirrors/air-gapped flows

Related:
- Linear: ENG-4821 (Private upgrade plan v1), PM-812 (GA requirements)
- Support: SUP-18488 (customer asking for rollback + clearer upgrade procedure)

## What changed
### 1) Version pinning
New variables on the Private modules:
- `redwood_private_version` (string, optional): pins the platform version deployed by the module
- `redwood_private_channel` (string, optional, default "stable"): allows future channel selection without breaking interfaces

Behavior:
- If `redwood_private_version` is set, we pass it through to the installer/helm values and include it in outputs.
- If unset, the module behaves as before (uses the default recommended version path defined by the module release).

### 2) Upgrade window inputs
Adds optional upgrade window variables:
- `upgrade_window_start` (string, optional, RFC3339 local-time-like "HH:MM" form)
- `upgrade_window_end` (string, optional)
- `upgrade_window_timezone` (string, optional, default "UTC")

Validation:
- If any of the window vars are set, all required parts must be set.
- Rejects obvious invalid formats (e.g., missing leading zeros) using `validation { condition = ... }`.

Note: Terraform cannot perfectly validate timezone names across providers without extra deps; we validate non-empty and document expected IANA tz database values.

### 3) Artifact source configuration (online)
Adds variables to make artifact sources explicit:
- `artifact_registry` (string, optional): container registry hostname / base
- `artifact_chart_repo` (string, optional): helm repo base URL
- `artifact_image_pull_secret_name` (string, optional): existing secret name (no secret material in TF state)

Defaults preserve current behavior (Redwood-hosted endpoints).

## Implementation notes
- All new inputs are plumbed through module outputs so operators can inspect effective settings from Terraform state.
- Added minimal guardrails to avoid storing credentials: we only accept secret *names* and rely on cluster-side secret creation.
- Updated docs and examples to show pinning + window configuration.

### Example usage
```
module "redwood_private" {
  source = "github.com/redwoodinference/redwood-terraform//modules/private-vpc"

  redwood_private_version   = "1.14.3"
  redwood_private_channel   = "stable"

  upgrade_window_start      = "02:00"
  upgrade_window_end        = "04:00"
  upgrade_window_timezone   = "America/Los_Angeles"
```

title: Private: add version pinning, upgrade window controls, and artifact source configuration
preview:
## Summary
This PR adds explicit Terraform inputs for Redwood Private lifecycle management: (1) version pinning (N-1 → N upgrade default), (2) upgrade window configuration to encode operator intent and reduce accidental upgrades outside of maintenance windows, and (3) artifact source configuration to make artifact provenance explicit (registry/chart repo URLs) and unblock future offline mirror work.

These changes are intentionally additive and default to today’s behavior when unset.

## Motivation
We’re rolling out a safer, more deterministic Private upgrade workflow (plan + prechecks + backup/rollback). Terraform is a primary entry point for VPC Private installs, and we need a consistent way to:
- Pin the target Redwood Private platform version across environments
- Avoid accidental upgrades by making a maintenance window an explicit input (and validated when provided)
- Standardize where artifacts come from so the installer can validate availability and we can later support mirrors/air-gapped flows

Related:
- Linear: ENG-4821 (Private upgrade plan v1), PM-812 (GA requirements)
- Support: SUP-18488 (customer asking for rollback + clearer upgrade procedure)

## What changed
### 1) Version pinning
New variables on the Private modules:
- `redwood_private_version` (string, optional): pins the platform version deployed by the module
- `redwood_private_channel` (string, optional, default "stable"): allows future channel selection without breaking interfaces

Behavior:
- If `redwood_private_version` is set, we pass it through to the installer/helm values and include it in outputs.
- If unset, the module behaves as before (uses the default recommended version path defined by the module release).

### 2) Upgrade window inputs
Adds optional upgrade window variables:
- `upgrade_window_start` (string, optional, RFC3339 local-time-like "HH:MM" form)
- `upgrade_window_end` (string, optional)
- `upgrade_window_timezone` (string, optional, default "UTC")

Validation:
- If any of the window vars are set, all required parts must be set.
- Rejects obvious invalid formats (e.g., missing leading zeros) using `validation { condition = ... }`.

Note: Terraform cannot perfectly validate timezone names across providers without extra deps; we validate non-empty and document expected IANA tz database values.

### 3) Artifact source configuration (online)
Adds variables to make artifact sources explicit:
- `artifact_registry` (string, optional): container registry hostname / base
- `artifact_chart_repo` (string, optional): helm repo base URL
- `artifact_image_pull_secret_name` (string, optional): existing secret name (no secret material in TF state)

Defaults preserve current behavior (Redwood-hosted endpoints).

## Implementation notes
- All new inputs are plumbed through module outputs so operators can inspect effective settings from Terraform state.
- Added minimal guardrails to avoid storing credentials: we only accept secret *names* and rely on cluster-side secret creation.
- Updated docs and examples to show pinning + window configuration.

```

  source = "github.com/redwoodinference/redwood-terraform//modules/private-vpc"

  redwood_private_version   = "1.14.3"
  redwood_private_channel   = "stable"

  upgrade_window_start      = "02:00"
  upgrade_window_end        = "04:00"
  upgrade_window_timezone   = "America/Los_Angeles"

  artifact_registry         = "registry.redwood.example.com"
  artifact_chart_repo       = "https://charts.redwood.example.com/private"
  artifact_image_pull_secret_name = "redwood-regcred"
}
```

- `modules/private-vpc/outputs.tf`: expose effective version/window/artifact settings
- `modules/private-vpc/locals.tf`: compute defaults and normalized values
- `modules/private-vpc/helm-values.tpl`: pass values into chart values for installer/CP gating
- `examples/private-vpc/`: add pinned-version + window example
- `docs/private/upgrade.md`: document recommended upgrade flow, pinning, and window semantics

- chore(private): add version pinning variables + outputs
- feat(private): add upgrade window variables with validation and docs
- feat(private): add artifact source inputs (registry/chart repo)
- docs(examples): update private-vpc examples

## Testing

- `tflint` (CI)

- No provider upgrades required
- Coordinated with Release Eng to ensure module release notes highlight pinned versions for design partners

- Tighten timezone validation once we standardize accepted tz list

source.title: Add Terraform modules for Dedicated burst pools (per region + hardware class)
source.author: Vanessa Ortiz
source.description:
Context: Dedicated Burst Capacity needs region/hardware-scoped “burst pools” that can be independently sized and rolled out (e.g., us-east/H100, eu-west/L40S), with clear safety controls and consistent tagging/monitoring. This PR adds reusable Terraform modules + environment wiring to provision burst pools per region/hardware class behind allow-lists, and to standardize alerts/labels used by the admission controller + SRE dashboards.

What’s in this PR: (1) New module: modules/burst_pool (GPU pool + autoscaler + quotas/labels). The module provisions a dedicated node pool / capacity group for “managed burst” with explicit isolation labels/taints so only burst workloads land there. Supports both “shared burst pool” (multi-tenant) and “segmented burst pool” (by tier) via a pool_id + tier label. (2) New module: modules/burst_pool_observability to attach baseline monitors and alert routes (capacity saturation, provisioning failures, node readiness, and a conservative fleet safety cap indicator exported as a metric). (3) Environment wiring: envs/{staging,prod}/{region}/{hw_class}/burst_pools.tf with a standardized map-driven configuration: enablement flag, target_min/target_max, safety_buffer_percent, and rollout_allowlist for region/hw class. (4) Standard tagging and cost allocation: tags include product=dedicated, feature=burst, region, hw_class, pool_id, and owner=fleet-capacity. Adds per-pool cost center tag to match Finance’s reporting needs. (5) Outputs/inputs for downstream: exports pool identifiers and labels required by the admission controller and routing (pool_name, pool_labels, taint_keys, capacity_class). This is intentionally a minimal interface; admission control policy still lives in the control plane.

Guardrails / rollout: Defaults are safe: burst pools are disabled everywhere unless explicitly enabled per env + per region/hw class allowlist. For prod, the initial plan is to enable only us-east/H100 and us-west/H100 in a follow-up apply once sizing is signed off (see ENG-4902). The module includes a hard “max_total_gpus” cap and requires explicit acknowledgement var (ack_burst_pool_cost_risk=true) in prod to prevent accidental large scale-ups.

Testing: CI runs terraform fmt/validate + tflint. This PR also adds a lightweight “plan matrix” job for staging envs (no apply) to ensure module composition is valid across region/hw variants.

Notes / follow-ups: (a) We intentionally do NOT support cross-region bursting here; pools are created and consumed within the same region. (b) We’re not wiring customer-specific pools in this initial pass; segmentation is only by tier labels to support fairness experiments while still being Redwood-managed. (c) We’ll add the first prod enablement + capacity numbers in a separate PR after final sizing review and procurement confirmation.

References: ENG-4902 fleet safety cap + global circuit breaker work; PM-512 design direction for burst pool topology; SRE alerting thresholds to be tuned post dogfood.
commits: modules/burst_pool: initial burst pool module (node pool + labels/taints + autoscaler hooks)
modules/burst_pool_observability: add baseline alerts and dashboard annotations
envs: add burst_pools.tf wiring for staging/prod with allow-list + safe defaults
ci: add terraform plan matrix for staging regions/hardware classes
docs: add module README and example usage
notable_snippets: Example usage (staging/us-east/h100/burst_pools.tf): module "burst_pool_us_east_h100" { source = "../../../modules/burst_pool" region = "us-east" hw_class = "h100" pool_id = "shared" enabled = var.enable_burst_pools && contains(var.burst_pool_allowlist, "us-east/h100") target_min_gpus = 32 target_max_gpus = 128 safety_buffer_percent = 15 max_total_gpus = 160 labels = { "redwood.ai/capacity-class" = "burst" "redwood.ai/burst-pool" = "shared" } taints = [{ key = "redwood.ai/burst-only" value = "true" effect = "NO_SCHEDULE" }] tags = local.standard_tags }

Prod guardrail (modules/burst_pool/variables.tf): variable "ack_burst_pool_cost_risk" { type = bool default = false description = "Required in prod to enable burst pools (prevents accidental GPU scale-ups)." }
Enablement check (modules/burst_pool/main.tf): locals { effective_enabled = var.enabled && (var.environment != "prod" || var.ack_burst_pool_cost_risk) }
review_conversation: 2025-03-12 Keiko Yamamoto: Can we make sure the taint key is stable and shared across regions? The admission controller and runtime scheduling will rely on it. Also, please confirm autoscaler max nodes can’t exceed the fleet safety cap var.

2025-03-12 Vanessa Ortiz (author): Yep. Updated to use redwood.ai/burst-only across all envs and exported the taint key via outputs. Also added max_total_gpus + a policy check to fail plan if target_max_gpus > max_total_gpus.

2025-03-13 Sean Gallagher: For observability: can we emit an annotation/tag that makes it easy to correlate pool_id + hw_class in dashboards? Also, alert severity—paging vs ticket—should match the SLO gate approach. Right now everything is “page.”

2025-03-13 Vanessa Ortiz (author): Good call. Added pool_id + hw_class tags everywhere + a dashboard annotation label. Changed default alert routing: saturation warnings -> ticket; provisioning failures and node readiness -> page (staging is always ticket).

2025-03-14 Irene Choi: Prod guardrail looks good. One more: please ensure prod defaults to enabled=false even if vars are mis-set. I’ve seen env merges go wrong. Can we require allowlist + ack var both?
2025-03-14 Vanessa Ortiz (author): Done. prod effective_enabled now requires (enabled && ack && allowlisted). Added a unit-ish check in CI that plan fails if enabled in prod without allowlist.

2025-03-17 Sergio Costa: Minor: naming conventions for alerts/recording rules should match the burst metrics taxonomy (prefix with burst_pool_). I see a couple resources named burst-saturation without prefix.
2025-03-17 Vanessa Ortiz (author): Renamed to burst_pool_saturation and burst_pool_node_ready to match the taxonomy. Pushed update; CI green.
2025-03-18 Keiko Yamamoto: Approved. Thanks for the explicit prod ack and allowlist gating.
2025-03-18 Sean Gallagher: Approved. Please link the runbook in the alert annotations once it exists (follow-up OK).
2025-03-18 Irene Choi: Approved.
source.release_notes: Infra only: introduces Terraform modules/env wiring for burst pool provisioning per region/hardware class behind allow-lists and prod guardrails; adds baseline observability resources and standardized tagging/outputs for downstream services.

source.title: Add retention policy config resources/modules for Private deployments
source.author: Vivek Kulkarni
source.description: Summary: Adds Terraform resources and a small helper module to configure retention controls for Redwood Private deployments where the control plane supports the retention policy service (policy schema v1.1). This is intended to reduce Security/GRC friction during enterprise onboarding by making retention configuration auditable, repeatable, and exportable. Motivation: Several Private design partners requested “infrastructure as code” for retention defaults and per-tenant overrides to align with internal change control. This PR wires up policy CRUD via the retention policy API and provides safe-by-default validation consistent with the control plane schema (min/max TTLs per tier enforced server-side; Terraform additionally validates basic shape for faster feedback). Scope: - New resource: redwood_retention_policy (create/update/delete + read) - New resource: redwood_retention_policy_attachment (attach policy to tenant/logging config where supported) - New data source: redwood_retention_policy (lookup by name/id) - New module: modules/retention_policy_private (opinionated defaults + examples) - Docs: provider docs + examples for common regulated profiles (short request-log retention + longer audit-log retention) Behavior notes: - Private-only: resources require provider config `deployment_mode = "private"` (or equivalent) and will error clearly in Hosted/Dedicated. - Legal hold is intentionally not modeled as a Terraform switch in v1; holds are handled via break-glass workflow and audited operations (see Security guidance). - Deletions are “policy object deletion” only; actual data purge is handled by server-side TTL enforcement jobs. source.title: Add NFS backup backend example + mount option guardrails
source.author: Bruno Silva
source.description:
Summary: This PR adds a first-class Terraform example for configuring NFS-backed backups for Redwood Private, and introduces input validation guardrails to prevent insecure NFS mount options from being used in production-like environments.

Context / Why: We’re seeing an increasing number of on-prem / restricted customers (and a few VPC installs) asking for NFS as their backup artifact store (either because object storage is not available, or because backup traffic must remain fully local). In parallel, we’ve had a few staging misconfigs where the backup CronJob mounted an NFS share with overly-permissive mount options (e.g., allowing suid/dev/exec), which is a footgun for environments that run backup tooling with elevated privileges. This PR is intended to (a) provide a reference implementation customers can copy/paste and (b) enforce sane defaults / fail-fast validation.

What’s included:
- New example: examples/private-backup-nfs/
  - Creates a Kubernetes namespace + secret + values snippet for Redwood’s backup CronJob integration, wired to an NFS PersistentVolume/PersistentVolumeClaim using the NFS CSI driver.
  - Documents the operational requirements we cannot enforce via Terraform (server-side export options like root_squash, network ACLs/firewalls, and backups retention/rotation).
- Guardrails in module inputs:
  - Adds validation for var.nfs_mount_options and var.backup_storage_backend when backend == "nfs".
  - Denies known-unsafe client mount options by default:
    - Disallow: "suid", "dev", "exec" (forces nodev,nosuid,noexec posture)
    - Disallow: "intr" (deprecated / confusing semantics) and "soft" (risk of silent data corruption / partial writes)
    - Strongly recommends (docs + default): "hard", "timeo=600", "retrans=2", "vers=4.1"
  - Adds a toggle var.allow_legacy_nfs (default false) to allow NFSv3 only when explicitly required; when true we allow "vers=3" but still require hard mounts and deny suid/dev/exec.
  - Adds validation that the mount options include nodev,nosuid,noexec unless var.allow_insecure_mount_options is explicitly set (default false). This keeps the “break glass” path available for truly constrained environments while forcing an intentional choice.
- Documentation updates:
  - README: Adds a “NFS backend” section, including recommended server export configuration, networking guidance, and a troubleshooting checklist (permission denied, stale file handle, clock skew).

Out of scope / non-goals:
- We do not attempt to configure NFS servers/appliances (that’s customer-specific). For AWS-native NFS, EFS is covered separately; this example is intentionally generic.
- We do not validate server-side export options (e.g., root_squash) because that is not observable from the client side.
- We do not handle application-level backup orchestration here; this example is only about storage plumbing + guardrails.

Implementation notes:
- The validations are implemented via Terraform variable validation blocks to keep failures at plan time.
- The example uses the Kubernetes provider for PV/PVC creation (since many on-prem clusters already run the NFS CSI driver). If customers use a different NFS provisioner, they can still reuse the values + mount options guidance.

Testing:
- terraform fmt / validate / tflint: PASS
- Example plan on kind + NFS CSI driver: PASS (smoke: PV/PVC bind + pod can read/write)
- Negative tests (manual): PASS
  - Setting mountOptions=["exec"] fails validation
  - Setting backend="nfs" without nodev/nosuid/noexec fails unless allow_insecure_mount_options=true
  - Setting vers=3 fails unless allow_legacy_nfs=true

Rollout / Compatibility:
- No breaking changes for existing users unless they were previously passing unsafe mount options (which we want to stop). If you hit validation failures, the recommended fix is to remove suid/dev/exec and add nodev,nosuid,noexec.
- The new variables are additive. Existing configs that do not set backend="nfs" are unaffected.

Security considerations:
- Guardrails are aligned with the Private threat model: backup jobs may run with elevated permissions; mounting an NFS volume with exec/suid/dev increases the blast radius of a compromised container or supply-chain event.
- The example explicitly recommends isolating the NFS export to the backup namespace/service account usage and restricting network access to cluster nodes.

Release notes:
- Adds NFS backend example for Private backup storage.
- Adds plan-time validation for NFS mount options to prevent insecure configurations.

Checklist:
- [x] terraform fmt
- [x] terraform validate
- [x] docs updated
- [x] security review requested (eng-security)

Commits:
- 4c2c0a1 examples: add private-backup-nfs reference config
- 91b7f44 module: add nfs mount option validation + legacy toggle
- 2f3b6f9 docs: README updates (NFS backend guidance)
- 8a9dd12 chore: add basic negative test notes to examples

Changed areas (high level):
- modules/private-backup/variables.tf
- modules/private-backup/README.md
- examples/private-backup-nfs/{main.tf,variables.tf,README.md,values/backup-cronjob-values.yaml}
- examples/private-backup-nfs/manifests/{pv.yaml,pvc.yaml}

Illustrative snippet (variables.tf):
- Added validation to reject unsafe mount options (exec/dev/suid/soft) and require nodev,nosuid,noexec by default; introduced allow_legacy_nfs and allow_insecure_mount_options.

Review conversation (abridged):
- Marcus Lin (Security): “+1 on denying exec/dev/suid. Please also call out why ‘soft’ is dangerous (partial writes) and make the ‘break glass’ variable name explicit.”
  - Bruno Silva: “Updated docs + renamed variable to allow_insecure_mount_options; added rationale for soft/intr.”
- Hanae Suzuki (Security/KMS): “Can we default to vers=4.1 and require an explicit toggle for v3? We have customers where v3 is the only option but we should make it deliberate.”
  - Bruno Silva: “Added allow_legacy_nfs=false and validation around vers=3.”
- Sean Gallagher (SRE): “Please add troubleshooting notes for RBAC vs NFS permissions; we keep mixing these up in on-call.”
  - Bruno Silva: “Added a troubleshooting section that distinguishes RBAC ‘forbidden’ from NFS ‘permission denied’ and notes common UID/GID mapping issues.”
- Connor O’Brien (Release): “Any risk this blocks existing customers?”
  - Bruno Silva: “Only if they were passing explicitly unsafe mount options; intent is to fail fast. Added migration note + ‘break glass’ toggle for constrained envs.”

CI checks:
- terraform-fmt: pass
- terraform-validate: pass
- tflint: pass
- docs-link-check: pass

Merge outcome: Merged (squash) into main on 2025-02-24 by Connor O’Brien.

source.title: Add S3+KMS backup module for Redwood Private (bucket, key, least-privilege IAM) + examples
source.author: Bruno Silva
source.description: Context: Private Day-2 backup/restore requires a supported, repeatable storage + encryption + IAM pattern for VPC deployments. This PR adds a Terraform module that provisions an S3 bucket configured for encrypted backup artifacts (SSE-KMS), a dedicated KMS key (optionally using an existing key), and a least-privilege IAM policy intended for the backup/restore job/installer identity. The module is designed to be "drop-in" for enterprise VPC environments with restricted IAM, and to align with the backup artifact layout produced by `redwood-private-installer backup create` (manifest + encrypted blob). What’s included: (1) module `modules/private_backup_s3_kms/` with S3 bucket, bucket policy options, versioning/lifecycle hooks, and KMS key + alias; (2) IAM policy document for backup writers/readers scoped to the bucket and a configurable prefix (default `redwood-private/backups/`), including required KMS permissions; (3) example usage under `examples/private-backup-s3-kms/` showing how to wire the policy to an IAM role (IRSA-friendly) and how to pass the bucket/prefix into Helm values; (4) README docs: assumptions, required inputs, hardening guidance, and common misconfigurations seen in customer accounts. Security posture: bucket has public access block enabled; encryption at rest enforced via default SSE-KMS and (optionally) a deny on `s3:PutObject` without `s3:x-amz-server-side-encryption`; KMS key policy supports granting to a dedicated backup role and supports rotation; recommended conditions included (kms:ViaService for S3, encryption context notes). Out of scope: cross-region replication, multi-account backup centralization, and server access logs destination bucket (doc note added). Testing: `terraform fmt`, `terraform validate`, and tflint; example plan runs in CI with mocked variables (no AWS calls).

notable_snippets: IAM policy scope: s3:ListBucket is limited to the bucket and conditioned on the configured prefix; object actions (GetObject/PutObject/DeleteObject) are limited to arn:aws:s3:::${bucket}/${prefix}*.

KMS permissions: kms:Encrypt/kms:Decrypt/kms:GenerateDataKey* limited to the module key ARN; optional condition added for kms:ViaService = s3.${region}.amazonaws.com when using the module-managed key.
Optional bucket policy hardening: deny PutObject when x-amz-server-side-encryption != aws:kms (configurable with var `enforce_sse_kms = true`).
